> This is the same equation appearing in the Formula Sheet (Row 1). The **negative sign** indicates that an opposing die pressure reduces the throughput. KTU examiners frequently test this derivation for full 7-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Layer Thickness in Co-extrusion (Feedblock Model)

Consider an **A/B/A** (skin/core/skin) co-extrusion with three layers. The total flow rate entering the feedblock is:

$$ Q_{total} = Q_A + Q_B + Q_A = 2Q_A + Q_B $$

After leaving the feedblock, the layer thickness is directly proportional to the flow rate of that layer (assuming equal channel width and density).

### Step 1 — Skin Layer Thickness

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{Q_{total}} $$

### Step 2 — Core Layer Thickness

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_B}{Q_{total}} $$

### Step 3 — Combine

Substituting $Q_{total} = 2Q_A + Q_B$:

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_B}{2 Q_A + Q_B} $$

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{2 Q_A + Q_B} $$

### Step 4 — Special Case (Equal Volumes of Skin and Core)
When $Q_A = Q_B$:

$$ L_A = \frac{L_{total}}{3}, \quad L_B = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

Wait — let me recompute. With $Q_A = Q_B$ and total $Q = 2Q_A + Q_B = 3Q_A$:

$$ L_A = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{3 Q_A} = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

$$ L_B = L_{total} \times \frac{Q_A}{3 Q_A} = \frac{L_{total}}{3} $$

Total skin contribution: $2 \times L_{total}/3 = 2L_{total}/3$ ✓  
Core contribution: $L_{total}/3$ ✓  
Sum: $L_{total}$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Foam Expansion Ratio in Physical Blowing Agent Extrusion

The **expansion ratio** $\phi$ is defined as the ratio of the solid (unfoamed) polymer density $\rho_s$ to the foamed polymer density $\rho_f$.

### Step 1 — Mass Conservation
For a fixed mass of polymer $m$:

$$ m = \rho_s V_s = \rho_f V_f $$

### Step 2 — Definition of Expansion Ratio

$$ \phi = \frac{V_f}{V_s} = \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f} $$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Blowing Agent Loading
If $m_g$ grams of gas dissolves in mass $m_p$ of polymer, the foamed volume includes the gas volume $V_g$ (ideal gas) at the foaming conditions:

$$ V_g = \frac{n_g R T}{P} = \frac{m_g}{M_g} \cdot \frac{R T}{P} $$

The total foam volume is $V_f = V_s + V_g$ (assuming gas escapes isothermally and isothermally), giving:

$$ \phi = 1 + \frac{V_g}{V_s} = 1 + \frac{\rho_s \, m_g \, R T}{M_g \, m_p \, P} $$

> [!TIP]
> This last form is **excellent for KTU numericals** because it directly links the *amount of blowing agent dosed* to the *final foam density* — a typical 7-mark question pattern.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Operation

| Criterion | Co-extrusion | Reactive Extrusion | Foam Extrusion | Film / Sheet |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary equipment** | Multiple extruders + die | Twin-screw extruder | Single/twin-screw with cooling | Single-screw + die |
| **Number of polymer streams** | ≥ 2 | 1 (+ reagents) | 1 (+ blowing agent) | 1 |
| **Key output property** | Layered structure | Modified chemistry | Cellular morphology | Gauge uniformity |
| **Reactor analogy** | None | Continuous stirred tank | Pressure-quench unit | Heat exchanger |
| **Major challenge** | Interfacial instability | Residence time control | Cell collapse | Neck-in, draw resonance |
| **Typical throughput** | 50 – 1500 kg/h | 5 – 500 kg/h | 20 – 800 kg/h | 50 – 2000 kg/h |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Co-extrusion Line

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional interactions of a **three-layer A/B/A co-extrusion line** from polymer feed to wound roll. It traces the path of materials, signals, and control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hopper A1 Skin Polymer] --> B1[Extruder A1 Melting Unit]
    A2[Hopper A2 Core Polymer] --> B2[Extruder A2 Melting Unit]
    A3[Hopper A3 Skin Polymer] --> B3[Extruder A3 Melting Unit]
    B1 --> C1[Gear Pump A1 Precise Metering]
    B2 --> C2[Gear Pump A2 Precise Metering]
    B3 --> C3[Gear Pump A3 Precise Metering]
    C1 --> D[Feedblock Layer Stacking Manifold]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Co-extrusion Die Multi Layer Exit]
    E --> F[Chill Roll Stack Temperature Controlled]
    F --> G[Thickness Gauge Online Measurement]
    G --> H[Edge Trimmer and Slitter Unit]
    H --> I[Winder Dual Station Turret]
    F --> J[Air Knife Edge Bead Removal]
    G -.->|Feedback Signal| K[PLC Central Controller]
    C1 -.->|Speed Trim| K
    C2 -.->|Speed Trim| K
    C3 -.->|Speed Trim| K
    K -.->|Command| F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent the **flow of material**; dashed arrows represent the **flow of control signals**. The PLC receives thickness feedback and dynamically adjusts the gear-pump speeds and chill-roll speed to maintain layer uniformity.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reactive Extrusion Line

The diagram below illustrates a **co-rotating twin-screw reactive extrusion** setup for maleic-anhydride grafting of polypropylene.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[PP Powder Feed Hopper] --> S1[Feeder Loss in Weight Gravimetric]
    S1 --> Z1[Zone 1 Solid Conveying]
    Z1 --> Z2[Zone 2 Melting and Plasticating]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 Side Feed of MAH and Peroxide Initiator]
    Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4 Reaction Zone High Shear Kneading]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone 5 Vacuum Degassing Volatile Removal]
    Z5 --> Z6[Zone 6 Metering and Pressure Build Up]
    Z6 --> DIE[Strand Die Multi Hole]
    DIE --> W1[Water Bath Quench Tank]
    W1 --> W2[Air Knife Surface Drying]
    W2 --> W3[Pelletizer Strand Cutter]
    W3 --> W4[Classification Sieve]
    W4 --> W5[Finished Grafted PP Bag]
    Z4 -.->|RTD Sensor| CTRL[Process Controller]
    Z5 -.->|Vacuum Pressure| CTRL
    CTRL -.->|Screw Speed| S1
    CTRL -.->|Initiator Feed| Z3
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow: Foam Extrusion (Physical Blowing Agent)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POLY[Polymer Pellets Feed] --> ME[Melt Extruder]
    ME --> MIX[Static Mixer Melt Homogenization]
    GAS[Supercritical CO2 Supply] --> INJ[Injection Port High Pressure]
    INJ --> MIX
    MIX --> COOL[Cooling Zone Temperature Drop]
    COOL --> DIE[Die Sudden Pressure Drop]
    DIE --> NUC[Cell Nucleation and Growth]
    NUC --> SHAPE[Shaping and Calibration Unit]
    SHAPE --> CUT[Sawing and Stacking]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Concept Embedded in Diagram:** The **pressure drop** in the die is what triggers nucleation. If the cooling zone is too cold, viscosity becomes too high and the gas cannot expand; if too warm, the cells coalesce and collapse. This balance is the heart of foam extrusion.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Operation Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COEX[Co-extrusion Control]
        CE[Layer Gauge Sensor] --> CPE[PLC]
        CPE --> CE1[Gear Pump Trim]
        CPE --> CE2[Chill Roll Trim]
    end
    subgraph REX[Reactive Extrusion Control]
        RE[RTD Probe] --> RPE[PLC]
        RPE --> RE1[Screw RPM Trim]
        RPE --> RE2[Initiator Feed Trim]
    end
    subgraph FOAM[Foam Extrusion Control]
        FE[Pressure Sensor at Die] --> FPE[PLC]
        FPE --> FE1[Gas Injection Trim]
        FPE --> FE2[Cooling Zone Trim]
    end
    subgraph FILM[Film Extrusion Control]
        FI[Gauge Scanner] --> FIE[PLC]
        FIE --> FI1[Haul-off Speed]
        FIE --> FI2[Die Lip Bolt Heaters]
    end
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**
**Q:** Define *reactive extrusion*. List **any two** types of chemical reactions that can be performed in a reactive extruder.

**Model Answer:**
Reactive extrusion (REX) is a continuous process in which one or more chemical reactions (polymerization, grafting, crosslinking, degradation) are carried out on the polymer melt inside an extruder barrel, typically a co-rotating twin-screw extruder. The extruder thus functions simultaneously as a **conveyor, mixer, heat exchanger, and chemical reactor**.

Two types of reactions:
1. **Grafting** – e.g., grafting of maleic anhydride onto polypropylene to produce a compatibilizer.
2. **Crosslinking** – e.g., peroxide-induced crosslinking of polyethylene (XLPE) for wire and cable insulation.

> [!TIP]
> *Examiner's Note:* Always state the equipment type (twin-screw) and a *specific example reaction*. Generic answers like "chemical modification" fetch only partial credit.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q:** What is the *blow-up ratio (BUR)* in blown film extrusion? State its typical range and explain how it affects film properties.

**Model Answer:**
The blow-up ratio (BUR) is the ratio of the **final bubble diameter** to the **die diameter**:

$$ \text{BUR} = \frac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}} $$

Typical BUR values lie between **1.5:1 and 4:1** for conventional LDPE/LLDPE films.

**Effect on properties:**
- Increasing BUR increases **transverse direction (TD) orientation**, improving TD tensile strength and tear resistance.
- Higher BUR generally reduces the tendency for **draw resonance** and neck-in.
- Excessive BUR (above 4:1) may cause **bubble instability** and non-uniform gauge.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** from each internal-choice block.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Co-extrusion & Film Operations

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a three-layer A/B/A co-extrusion line**. Discuss the function of each unit and explain the role of the *feedblock* and *multi-manifold die*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A three-layer A/B/A co-extrusion line consists of **three extruders**, three **gear pumps**, a **feedblock**, a **multi-layer die**, a **chill roll stack**, a **thickness gauge**, and a **winder**.

- *Extruders 1 and 3* melt the **skin (A) polymer** — typically a structural or barrier grade.
- *Extruder 2* melts the **core (B) polymer** — usually a cheaper, general-purpose grade.
- *Gear pumps* deliver **precise, pulsation-free flow rates** to the feedblock.
- The *feedblock* stacks the melts in the sequence A → B → A using internal flow channels.
- The *multi-manifold die* flattens and shapes the combined stream into a sheet or film.
- The *chill roll* rapidly solidifies the extrudate, locking the layer structure.
- The *thickness gauge* (often a beta or X-ray scanner) sends feedback to the PLC.
- The PLC adjusts gear-pump speeds and chill-roll RPM to maintain **layer-thickness tolerance** within ±2 %.

The **feedblock** is preferred for symmetric layer distribution; the **multi-manifold die** is preferred for wide sheets or asymmetric layer constructions.

**(b)** A three-layer co-extruded sheet has a total thickness of **1.2 mm**. The flow rates are $Q_A = 6 \, \text{kg/h}$ (skin), $Q_B = 12 \, \text{kg/h}$ (core), and $Q_A = 6 \, \text{kg/h}$ (other skin). Assuming equal densities, calculate the thickness of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Flow Rate**

$$ Q_{total} = 2 Q_A + Q_B = 2(6) + 12 = 24 \, \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Fraction of Each Layer (Mass fraction = Thickness fraction for equal densities)**

$$ f_{skin} = \frac{Q_A}{Q_{total}} = \frac{6}{24} = 0.25 $$

$$ f_{core} = \frac{Q_B}{Q_{total}} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.50 $$

**Step 3 — Thickness of Each Skin Layer**

$$ L_{skin} = 0.25 \times 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 0.30 \, \text{mm} $$

**Step 4 — Thickness of Core Layer**

$$ L_{core} = 0.50 \times 1.2 \, \text{mm} = 0.60 \, \text{mm} $$

**Final Layer Structure (A/B/A):** 0.30 mm / 0.60 mm / 0.30 mm ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students calculate the skin layer as 0.60 mm (forgetting the two skins sum to 0.60 mm, *not* 0.30 mm). The correct individual skin thickness is 0.30 mm. **Read the question carefully** — "thickness of each skin layer" means per-skin, not combined.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Reactive & Foam Extrusion

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of foam extrusion** with a labeled process flow diagram. Differentiate between **chemical blowing agents (CBA)** and **physical blowing agents (PBA)**. State two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Foam extrusion is a process in which a **blowing agent** is dissolved in the polymer melt inside the extruder, and a **sudden pressure drop** (usually at the die exit) triggers **cell nucleation, growth, and stabilization** to produce a cellular polymer structure with significantly reduced density.

**Process Flow:**
*Polymer pellets → Melt extruder → Static mixer (for homogenization) → Blowing-agent injection → Cooling zone → Die (pressure drop) → Foam shaping → Calibration → Cutting.*

| Feature | Chemical Blowing Agent (CBA) | Physical Blowing Agent (PBA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Thermal decomposition releases gas | Phase change / expansion of a fluid |
| **State at injection** | Solid powder or masterbatch | Liquid or supercritical fluid |
| **Two examples** | Azodicarbonamide (ADC); Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃) | Carbon dioxide (CO₂); Nitrogen (N₂) |
| **Decomposition / activation temperature** | 160 °C – 220 °C (ADC); 100 °C – 140 °C (NaHCO₃) | Independent of temperature; pressure-driven |
| **Storage / handling** | Stable at room temperature | Requires pressurized storage |

**(b)** A foam extrusion line processes **20 kg/h** of polymer. The solid density is **900 kg/m³** and the foamed density is **75 kg/m³**. Calculate the **expansion ratio** and the **additional volume** of gas generated per hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate**

$$ \dot{m} = 20 \, \text{kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Expansion Ratio**

$$ \phi = \frac{\rho_{solid}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{900}{75} = 12 $$

**Step 3 — Volume of Solid Polymer per Hour**

$$ V_{solid} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{solid}} = \frac{20}{900} \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.0222 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

**Step 4 — Volume of Foam per Hour**

$$ V_{foam} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{foam}} = \frac{20}{75} \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} = 0.2667 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

**Step 5 — Additional Volume of Gas**

$$ \Delta V_{gas} = V_{foam} - V_{solid} = 0.2667 - 0.0222 = 0.2444 \, \text{m}^3/\text{h} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** The **expansion ratio is dimensionless**, not in m³/h. Students who write "the expansion ratio is 12 m³/h" lose 2 marks. The **unit of additional gas volume is m³/h**, not dimensionless. Mark the two distinctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 – Operations**

- [ ] **Co-extrusion** combines multiple polymers in a single die; viscosity ratio must be **0.5 – 2.0** to avoid layer inversion.
- [ ] **Feedblock dies** suit symmetric A/B/A films; **multi-manifold dies** suit wide sheets.
- [ ] **Reactive extrusion** uses **co-rotating twin-screw extruders** because of their self-wiping, high surface renewal, and degassing capability.
- [ ] The **Damköhler number ($Da$)** compares reaction rate to convective rate — high $Da$ means reaction-limited, low $Da$ means mixing-limited.
- [ ] **Foam extrusion** requires a *pressure drop* (die) or *temperature rise* to nucleate cells.
- [ ] **CBA** decomposes chemically; **PBA** changes phase. Both reduce density by the **expansion ratio** $\phi$.
- [ ] **Cast film** is characterized by **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)**; **blown film** by **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)**.
- [ ] The **screw characteristic equation** $Q = \alpha N - \beta P / \eta$ is the single most tested formula in Module 2.
- [ ] **Specific Energy Input (SEI)** in kWh/kg is the energy benchmark for all extrusion operations.
- [ ] Always state **units explicitly** in numerical answers; mark loss for missing units is a recurring KTU board complaint.
- [ ] For co-extrusion layer-thickness problems, **verify the symmetry** — symmetric A/B/A problems have two equal skin layers.
- [ ] For foam numericals, the **expansion ratio is dimensionless**; the **gas volume has units of m³/h or L**.
- [ ] Twin-screw extruders are *the* default for reactive and foam extrusion; single-screw extruders are *the* default for film and basic profile extrusion.
- [ ] **Interfacial instability** in co-extrusion manifests as wavy, broken, or encapsulated layers; controlled by **viscosity matching** and **shear stress balance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip:** When asked "compare" or "differentiate" two operations, always produce a **tabulated answer** with at least **four** comparison points. The KTU Board examiner's key explicitly awards **+1 mark** per valid comparison point, with the final **+1 mark** for a clear overall summary statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing of Pasta and Vermicelli

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing of Pasta and Vermicelli

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🍝 Manufacturing of Pasta and Vermicelli — KTU Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pasta** is a cereal-based, shaped, and dried food product manufactured by **cold extrusion** of a dough prepared primarily from **durum wheat semolina (Triticum durum)** and water, optionally with eggs (for egg noodles/pasta), through a die under high pressure, followed by cutting and controlled drying. Vermicelli is a sub-category of extruded pasta, characterized by a **very thin, elongated, cylindrical or ribbon-like geometry** (typical diameter **0.8–1.2 mm**) and is commonly manufactured from **rice flour, wheat flour, or a blend**, often with tapioca or starch additives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEFTT637 – Module 2):**
> *Pasta and vermicelli are the prime commercial examples of **low-pressure, low-temperature cold extrusion** of cereal doughs. Unlike thermoplastic cooking extrusion (90–200 °C, 2–20 MPa), pasta extrusion is conducted at temperatures below ~50 °C and pressures of 0.5–3 MPa, with the primary goal of **shaping** rather than cooking.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **garlic press**: you push a soft paste (dough) through tiny holes, and out comes dough shaped like spaghetti. A pasta extruder is essentially a **giant, industrial-scale, continuous garlic press** — except the paste is a carefully hydrated semolina dough, the holes (dies) are precision-engineered, and the resulting strands are then slowly dried to lock in shape and structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In pasta extrusion, *starch granules are not gelatinized* during shaping. The starch is gelatinized only during the consumer's cooking step. This is what makes pasta a **cold-formed**, shelf-stable product — and is the central reason pasta is classified as a cold-extrusion technology, not a hot cooking extrusion.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Semolina moisture (pre-mixing) | **30–32 % w/w** |
| Dough moisture at extruder inlet | **28–30 % w/w** |
| Extrusion temperature | **< 50 °C (cold extrusion)** |
| Extrusion pressure | **0.5 – 3 MPa** |
| Final dried moisture | **≤ 12.5 % w/w** |
| Drying temperature | **40 – 80 °C** |
| Drying duration (long pasta) | **18 – 36 hours** |
| Vermicelli diameter | **0.8 – 1.2 mm** |
| Vermicelli length | **5 – 25 cm** (cut) |
| Throughput (industrial) | **300 – 2000 kg/h** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Durum Wheat?** Durum wheat (Triticum durum) has a hard, vitreous endosperm with **~12–14 % protein** (mostly gluten) and a **high yellow pigment (lutein)** content. This gives pasta its characteristic **yellow color, firm texture (al dente bite), and superior cooking tolerance** — properties not achievable with soft bread wheat.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Raw Material Specification

The rheological and final-product quality of pasta is **dominated** by raw material composition.

#### 2.1.1 Durum Wheat Semolina
- **Particle size:** 250–350 µm (coarser than flour, retains granular structure).
- **Protein content:** minimum **10.5 % w/w** (higher protein = stronger gluten network = better cooking quality).
- **Ash content:** maximum **0.90 %** (regulatory limit for "semolina").
- **Gluten quality index:** > 60 (measures elasticity & tenacity).
- **Damaged starch:** < 7 % (excess damaged starch causes sticky, low-quality dough).

#### 2.1.2 Water
- Potable, free of chlorine (chlorine bleaches semolina pigments and degrades gluten).
- Optimal dough moisture: **28–32 % w/w**.

#### 2.1.3 Optional Ingredients
| Ingredient | Function | Typical Dose |
|---|---|---|
| Eggs (whole / yolk) | Nutrition, color, flavor, binding | 100–200 g egg/kg flour |
| Vegetable oil | Surface smoothness, reduce sticking | 0.5–1.5 % |
| Salt (NaCl) | Flavor, gluten strengthening | 0.5–2 % |
| Beta-carotene / turmeric | Color standardization | 0.005–0.01 % |
| Monoglycerides | Anti-sticking in drying | 0.5 % |

### 2.2 Unit Operations in Pasta Manufacturing

The pasta manufacturing line is a **continuous sequence of 7 unit operations**:

1. **Metering & Pre-mixing** of semolina and water
2. **Mixing / Hydration** (5–15 min, 25–40 °C)
3. **Vacuum Kneading** (under −0.8 bar to remove occluded air)
4. **Cold Extrusion** through shaped dies
5. **Cutting** to specified length
6. **Pre-drying & Drying** (controlled humidity chambers)
7. **Cooling & Packaging**

### 2.3 Mixing & Kneading — The Why and How

> **Why?** Hydration of semolina must be **uniform and complete** so that the gluten proteins (gliadin + glutenin) form a continuous viscoelastic matrix. Insufficient hydration → crumbly, weak dough; over-hydration → sticky, extrudate that sags.

> **How?** Mixing combines dry semolina with water in a **helical (paddle) mixer** for 5–10 minutes. The crumbly mix is then transferred to a **vacuum kneader (auger-type)** where it is worked into a homogeneous, plastic mass at **25–40 °C** for 8–15 minutes. The vacuum (−0.6 to −0.8 bar) removes air bubbles that would otherwise cause **white specks, surface cracks, and uneven drying**.

### 2.4 Cold Extrusion of Pasta

Extrusion in pasta is **purely a shaping (forming) operation**, NOT a cooking operation.

#### 2.4.1 Extruder Types
- **Continuous press (most common):** Single-screw extruder with a worm rotating inside a cylindrical barrel, terminating at a die head. Screw diameter 100–250 mm; L/D 4:1 to 8:1.
- **Pressa (batch press):** Hydraulic piston extrusion, used for specialty/sheet pasta (lasagna, cannelloni).

#### 2.4.2 Die Design
| Die Type | Product | Die Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| Circular holes (0.9–1.2 mm) | Spaghetti, vermicelli | Brass or PTFE inserts |
| Slotted dies (2–6 mm wide) | Fettuccine, tagliatelle | Flat orifice |
| Special insert dies | Penne, macaroni, rigatoni | Bore with or without teflon insert |

> **Die material:** **Teflon (PTFE)** or **bronze** — bronze gives a rough surface that holds sauce better; Teflon gives a smooth, glossy surface.

### 2.5 Drying — The Most Critical Step

> [!WARNING]
> **>50 % of pasta quality defects originate from incorrect drying.** Drying is the slowest and most energy-intensive step (up to **40 % of total plant energy**).

#### 2.5.1 Drying Principles
Drying occurs in **3 sequential phases**:

**Phase 1 — Surface moisture removal (Constant rate period):**
- Surface water evaporates rapidly.
- Product temperature is near wet-bulb temperature (~30–35 °C).
- Duration: 1–3 hours.

**Phase 2 — Falling rate period (Diffusion-controlled):**
- Internal moisture diffuses to surface.
- Product temperature rises gradually.
- **Most critical for preventing *checkers* (surface cracks).**

**Phase 3 — Final equilibration:**
- Product moisture equilibrates to **< 12.5 %**.
- Temperature 60–80 °C.

#### 2.5.2 Drying Curves

| Phase | Moisture Range | Temperature | Humidity | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-drying (Phase 1) | 30 % → 22 % | 35–45 °C | High (80–90 % RH) | 1–3 h |
| Main drying (Phase 2) | 22 % → 14 % | 45–65 °C | Stepwise decreasing | 10–20 h |
| Final drying (Phase 3) | 14 % → 12.5 % | 60–80 °C | 30–50 % RH | 4–8 h |
| Stabilization | 12.5 % | Cool to 25 °C | Conditioning | 1–2 h |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** All absolute value bars in formulas below are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table syntax breakage.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,solid}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (dry basis) | % |
| 2 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (wet basis) | % |
| 3 | $M_{wb} = \dfrac{100 \cdot M_{db}}{100 + M_{db}}$ | Conversion db → wb | % |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{water} = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot \dfrac{M_1 - M_2}{100}$ | Water to be removed (dry basis) | kg/h |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heat for evaporation | kW |
| 6 | $h_{fg}(T) = 2501 - 2.42\,T$ | Latent heat of vaporization of water | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^3}{16} \cdot \eta \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$ | Screw torque (Bühler-type) | N·m |
| 8 | $P_{screw} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau$ | Screw power | W |
| 9 | $\dot{V} = K \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\eta \cdot L}$ | Volumetric flow through die (Hagen-Poiseuille) | m³/s |
| 10 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot r^4}{8 \eta L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille equation (single die hole) | m³/s |
| 11 | $\tau_{wall} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$ | Wall shear stress in die | Pa |
| 12 | $\dot{m}_{product} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{semolina} \cdot (100 - M_i)}{(100 - M_f)}$ | Mass balance for drying | kg/h |
| 13 | $N_{die} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{product}}{\rho \cdot v_{extrudate} \cdot A_{die}}$ | Number of die holes required | — |
| 14 | $t_{drying} \approx \dfrac{L^2}{\pi^2 \cdot D_{eff}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{M_i - M_{eq}}{M_f - M_{eq}}\right)$ | Drying time (Fickian diffusion model) | s |
| 15 | $Re_{die} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot d}{\eta} < 10$ | Laminar flow criterion in die (always satisfied for pasta) | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Re < 10 in the die** is the **signature of cold extrusion flow regime**: highly laminar, viscous, with no inertial effects. This is why Hagen-Poiseuille (Equation 10) describes pasta extrusion so well.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial capacity:** A modern pasta line (e.g., Bühler, Pavan, Storci) processes **2–5 tonnes/h** of semolina and runs **22–24 h/day**.
- **Energy footprint:** Drying consumes ~**3.5–4.5 MJ/kg pasta** (≈ 1 kWh/kg); modern low-temperature drying cycles can reduce this by 30 %.
- **Market context:** Global pasta production exceeds **17 million tonnes/year** (2024 data, IGQ/IPO estimate), with vermicelli dominating in South and East Asian markets (~3.5 Mt/yr).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hagen–Poiseuille Flow Through a Pasta Die

We derive the volumetric flow rate of dough through a single circular die hole to predict extrusion throughput.

#### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a cylindrical die hole of:
- Radius: $r$ [m]
- Length: $L$ [m]
- Pressure drop across die: $\Delta P$ [Pa]
- Dough apparent viscosity: $\eta$ [Pa·s]

#### 3.1.2 Governing Equations
Assume **steady, laminar, incompressible, isothermal, fully-developed flow**. The Navier-Stokes equation in the axial ($z$) direction reduces to:

$$\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\eta}\dfrac{dP}{dz}$$

#### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Substitute the constant pressure gradient $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ :

$$\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{\eta L}$$

**Step 2** — First integration (multiply by $r$ and integrate):

$$r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\eta L}\,r^2 + C_1$$

**Step 3** — Apply boundary condition: at $r = 0$, $\dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = 0$ (symmetry). Therefore $C_1 = 0$ :

$$\dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\eta L}\,r$$

**Step 4** — Second integration:

$$v_z(r) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\,r^2 + C_2$$

**Step 5** — Apply no-slip BC: at $r = R$ (die wall), $v_z = 0$ :

$$C_2 = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4\eta L}$$

**Step 6** — Final velocity profile (parabolic):

$$v_z(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\eta L}\!\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Step 7** — Volumetric flow rate per hole (integrate over die cross-section):

$$\dot{V} = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \int_0^R \!\left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr$$

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L}\left[\dfrac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \dfrac{r^4}{4}\right]_0^R = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\eta L} \cdot \dfrac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P \cdot r^4}{8 \eta L}}$$

**Interpretation:** Throughput scales with the **4th power of die radius** — doubling the hole diameter increases flow by **16×**. This is why **die selection is the most sensitive throughput control knob** in pasta extrusion.

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Number of Die Holes for a Given Throughput

#### 3.2.1 Setup
We need to determine $N_{die}$ (number of holes) for a target hourly production of dried pasta at throughput $\dot{m}_{product}$ [kg/h].

**Given:** $A_{die} = \pi r^2$ [m²], $v_{extrudate}$ [m/s], $\rho_{dough}$ [kg/m³], $M_i$ = initial moisture, $M_f$ = final moisture.

#### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1** — Mass flow per die hole at the extruder exit (after die, before drying):

$$\dot{m}_{hole} = \rho_{dough} \cdot v_{extrudate} \cdot A_{die} = \rho \cdot v \cdot \pi r^2$$

**Step 2** — Account for moisture loss during drying (mass balance). Dry solid mass is conserved:

$$\dot{m}_{dry\,solid} = \dot{m}_{wet\,extrudate} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{M_i}{100}\right) = \dot{m}_{product} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{M_f}{100}\right)$$

**Step 3** — Solve for the wet extrudate mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_{wet} = \dot{m}_{product} \cdot \dfrac{(100 - M_f)}{(100 - M_i)}$$

**Step 4** — Number of die holes required:

$$N_{die} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{wet}}{\dot{m}_{hole}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{product} \cdot (100 - M_f)}{\rho \cdot v \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot (100 - M_i)}$$

$$\boxed{N_{die} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{product}}{\rho \cdot v \cdot \pi r^2} \cdot \dfrac{(100 - M_f)}{(100 - M_i)}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Pasta Spaghetti Line)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample problem (board-exam style):** Design a spaghetti die for a 1000 kg/h pasta line. Given: spaghetti diameter $d = 1.0$ mm, dough density $\rho = 1300$ kg/m³, extrudate velocity $v = 0.5$ m/s, initial moisture $M_i = 30\%$, final moisture $M_f = 12\%$.

**Step 1** — Hole radius: $r = 0.0005$ m

**Step 2** — Cross-section area of one hole:

$$A_{die} = \pi r^2 = \pi \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3** — Mass flow per hole (per second, then per hour):

$$\dot{m}_{hole} = 1300 \times 0.5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-7} = 5.105 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 1.838 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4** — Wet extrudate required:

$$\dot{m}_{wet} = 1000 \times \dfrac{(100 - 12)}{(100 - 30)} = 1000 \times \dfrac{88}{70} = 1257.1 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5** — Number of die holes:

$$N_{die} = \dfrac{1257.1}{1.838} = 683.95 \approx \mathbf{684 \text{ holes}}$$

**Step 6** — Verification: Industrial spaghetti die inserts typically contain **600–900 holes** of 1.0 mm diameter. The result **684** falls squarely in the industrial range. ✅

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pasta vs. Vermicelli

| Parameter | Pasta (Spaghetti/Macaroni) | Vermicelli |
|---|---|---|
| Primary raw material | Durum wheat semolina | Rice flour / wheat flour / tapioca blend |
| Particle size of flour | Coarse (250–350 µm) | Fine (100–180 µm) |
| Dough moisture | 28–32 % | 30–35 % |
| Screw configuration | Single screw, L/D 4–8 | Twin-screw often preferred for rice blends |
| Die type | Multi-hole round, slotted, or insert | Multi-hole round (0.8–1.2 mm) |
| Throughput per die hole | 1–3 kg/h | 0.3–1.0 kg/h |
| Drying cycle | 18–36 h, multi-stage | 4–10 h (thinner cross-section) |
| Final moisture | ≤ 12.5 % | ≤ 10–12 % |
| Cooking time (consumer) | 8–12 min | 3–5 min |
| Color | Yellow (lutein from durum) | White / cream (rice) or yellow (wheat) |
| Typical shelf life | 24–36 months | 9–12 months |

### 3.5 Critical Quality Parameters

1. **Cooking Loss (CL):** Weight loss of pasta solids into cooking water. Target: < **7 % w/w** (for high-quality durum pasta). Measured as:

$$CL = \dfrac{m_{dry\,pasta} - m_{cooked\,pasta\,(drained,\,dried)}}{m_{dry\,pasta}} \times 100$$

2. **Water Absorption (WA):** Hydration after cooking. Target: **1.6–2.0 g water/g pasta**.

$$WA = \dfrac{m_{cooked} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}$$

3. **Firmness / Al Dente Bite:** Sensory + texture analyzer; target **5–8 N bite force** at optimal cooking.

4. **Color (L\*, a\*, b\*):** L\* = 78–84, b\* = 18–24 (yellowness) for standard semolina pasta.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Process Flow: Pasta Manufacturing Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["RAW MATERIAL STORAGE"]
        SEM["Semolina / Flour Silo"]
        WAT["Water Tank"]
        ADD["Additives: Eggs, Salt, Oil"]
    end

    subgraph B["PREPARATION"]
        M1["Volumetric / Gravimetric Metering"]
        MX["Pre-Mixer (Helical)"]
        KN["Vacuum Kneader (-0.8 bar)"]
    end

    subgraph C["EXTRUSION"]
        EX1["Single-Screw Extruder"]
        DIE1["Pasta Die Head: Round / Slotted / Insert"]
        CUT["Rotary Cutter Blade"]
    end

    subgraph D["DRYING CHAIN"]
        PD1["Pre-Dryer 1: 40 C, 90 percent RH"]
        PD2["Pre-Dryer 2: 55 C, 70 percent RH"]
        MD["Main Dryer: 65 C, 50 percent RH"]
        FD["Final Dryer: 75 C, 30 percent RH"]
        STB["Stabilizer: 25 C, 1-2 h"]
    end

    subgraph E["FINISHING"]
        QC["QC: Moisture, Color, Length"]
        PK["Packaging: 500 g, 1 kg, 5 kg"]
    end

    SEM --> M1
    WAT --> M1
    ADD --> M1
    M1 --> MX --> KN
    KN --> EX1 --> DIE1 --> CUT
    CUT --> PD1 --> PD2 --> MD --> FD --> STB
    STB --> QC --> PK

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#8a6d00
    style B fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003d7a
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#7a0000
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006400
    style E fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#3d0066
```

### 4.2 Detailed Drying-Phase Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Extruded Wet Pasta 30 percent Moisture"] --> P1

    P1["PHASE 1: Constant Rate 1-3 h"]:::phase1
    P1 --> CHK1{"Checkers Crack Risk? Lower RH Slowly"}
    CHK1 -->|Yes, Reduce RH| P1
    CHK1 -->|No, Moisture at 22 percent| P2

    P2["PHASE 2: Falling Rate 10-20 h"]:::phase2
    P2 --> CHK2{"Internal Stress High? Use Stepwise RH Drop"}
    CHK2 -->|Yes| P2
    CHK2 -->|No, Moisture at 14 percent| P3

    P3["PHASE 3: Final Drying 4-8 h"]:::phase3
    P3 --> CHK3{"Moisture equals 12.5 percent?"}
    CHK3 -->|No| P3
    CHK3 -->|Yes| P4

    P4["STABILIZATION 1-2 h at 25 C"]:::stabil
    P4 --> OUT["Final Dried Pasta Storage"]

    classDef phase1 fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#b38600
    classDef phase2 fill:#ffd699,stroke:#b35900
    classDef phase3 fill:#ffbf80,stroke:#993d00
    classDef stabil fill:#b3e6b3,stroke:#006600
```

### 4.3 Die Geometry Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIE["DIE INSERT CLASSES"]
        RND["Round Hole 0.9-1.2 mm: Spaghetti, Vermicelli"]
        SLT["Slot 2-6 mm: Fettuccine, Tagliatelle"]
        SPC["Special Insert: Penne, Macaroni, Rigatoni"]
    end

    subgraph MAT["DIE MATERIALS"]
        BRZ["Bronze Die: Rough Surface, Better Sauce Grip"]
        TFL["Teflon PTFE Die: Smooth, Glossy, Easy Release"]
        MIX["Bronze-Plus-PTFE: Hybrid Performance"]
    end

    RND --> MAT
    SLT --> MAT
    SPC --> MAT
```

### 4.4 Mass & Energy Flow Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["INPUTS: Semolina 1000 kg, Water 350 kg"] --> MIX
    MIX["Mixing and Kneading (30 percent moisture)"] --> EXT
    EXT["Cold Extrusion (1.5 MPa, 40 C)"] --> CUT
    CUT["Cutting to Specified Length"] --> DRY
    DRY["Drying: Removes 263 kg Water"] --> OUT

    OUT["OUTPUTS: Dried Pasta 1087 kg at 12.5 percent moisture"]:::outbox
    VAP["Water Vapour 263 kg (Exhaust Air)"]:::vapour

    DRY --> VAP

    classDef outbox fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef vapour fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d7a,stroke-width:2px
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Why is pasta extrusion called "cold extrusion"? State any two distinguishing features compared to hot cooking extrusion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pasta extrusion is termed *cold* because the dough is shaped at temperatures below **50 °C** and pressures of **0.5–3 MPa**, well below the gelatinization temperature of starch (which occurs only during the consumer's cooking). Distinguishing features:
1. **Starch remains ungelatinized** at the die exit; gelatinization is deferred to the final cooking step.
2. **Product is post-extrusion dried, not cooked** — final moisture is reduced to **≤ 12.5 %** for shelf stability without thermal denaturation of the gluten network.
3. The extruder acts purely as a **forming device**, not as a chemical reactor (unlike cooking extruders which use shear, pressure, and 90–200 °C to gelatinize starch in-line).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *List the major raw materials used in vermicelli manufacture. Why is rice flour preferred over wheat flour in some vermicelli variants?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Raw materials:** Rice flour (or wheat flour), water, tapioca starch (optional binder), salt, permitted color/flavor additives.
**Why rice flour is preferred:**
1. **Gluten-free** — suitable for celiac consumers and Asian dietary preferences.
2. Produces a **translucent, soft, white** appearance in the finished product.
3. **Neutral flavor** that absorbs accompanying sauces/seasonings better.
4. **Shorter cooking time** (3–5 min) due to finer particle size and pre-gelatinized starch structure.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** With the aid of a process flow diagram, describe in detail the **manufacturing of pasta by cold extrusion**, covering raw material selection, mixing, kneading, extrusion, and cutting. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **principles of pasta drying** in a continuous industrial dryer. Discuss the three phases of drying and the cause and prevention of "checkers" (surface cracks). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Raw material selection (1.5 marks):** Durum wheat semolina with > 10.5 % protein, ash < 0.9 %, particle size 250–350 µm. Water 28–32 % moisture. Optional: eggs, salt, monoglycerides.
2. **Mixing (1 mark):** Helical paddle mixer combines semolina + water for 5–10 min at 25–40 °C, producing a uniform crumbly dough.
3. **Vacuum kneading (1 mark):** Auger-type kneader at −0.6 to −0.8 bar vacuum for 8–15 min → homogeneous, plastic, air-free dough.
4. **Cold extrusion (2 marks):** Single-screw extruder (L/D 4–8) with screw speed 25–40 rpm, barrel jacket 25–40 °C, pressure 0.5–3 MPa. Dough forced through die (bronze/Teflon) with 100–900 holes.
5. **Cutting (0.5 mark):** Rotary knife blade at die exit cuts strands to specified length (e.g., 25 cm spaghetti) and lays them on stick-spreader rods.
6. **Process flow:** Metering → Mixing → Kneading → Extrusion → Cutting → Spreading → Drying → Cooling → Packing.

**Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks**

1. **Why drying is critical (0.5 mark):** Drying reduces moisture from 30 % to < 12.5 %; this locks shape, prevents mold, and enables 2–3 year shelf life.
2. **Phase 1 — Constant rate period (2 marks):** Surface moisture evaporates freely; product temp = wet-bulb temp (~30 °C); high RH 80–90 % to prevent surface skin formation. Duration 1–3 h.
3. **Phase 2 — Falling rate period (2 marks):** Internal moisture diffuses to surface. Rate slows. Product temp rises to 50–65 °C. Stepwise RH reduction is critical.
4. **Phase 3 — Final drying (1 mark):** Moisture 14 % → 12.5 % at 60–80 °C, RH 30–50 %. Duration 4–8 h.
5. **Checkers (surface cracks) (1.5 marks):**
   - **Cause:** Surface dries faster than interior → tensile stress > surface tensile strength.
   - **Prevention:** Controlled RH ramp; long pre-drying; high initial RH (90 %).
   - **Detection:** Visual + light-box inspection.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **raw material and process differences** between pasta (durum semolina) and vermicelli (rice-based) manufacturing. Highlight the role of dough moisture and particle size. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** For an industrial spaghetti line, calculate the **number of die holes required** given: throughput 1500 kg/h dried pasta, die hole diameter 1.1 mm, dough density 1350 kg/m³, extrudate velocity 0.6 m/s, initial dough moisture 30 %, final pasta moisture 12.5 %. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Raw material difference (2 marks):** Pasta uses coarse durum semolina (250–350 µm) with high protein (10.5–14 %) and yellow pigment. Vermicelli uses **fine rice/wheat flour** (100–180 µm) with lower protein; tapioca starch often added for binding and gloss.
2. **Particle size impact (2 marks):** Coarse semolina hydrates slowly, requires longer kneading, and produces a **firmer extrudate**. Fine rice flour hydrates rapidly, giving a **softer, smoother, more cohesive dough** that is more easily shaped into very thin strands.
3. **Dough moisture (1.5 marks):** Vermicelli typically uses 30–35 % moisture (slightly higher) to compensate for low gluten; pasta uses 28–30 % because strong gluten network binds well at lower moisture.
4. **Process differences (1.5 marks):** Vermicelli often uses **twin-screw extruders** for better mixing of low-protein flour blends; pasta uses single-screw. Vermicelli dries faster (4–10 h) due to thinner cross-section; pasta long goods take 18–36 h.

**Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{product} = 1500$ kg/h, $d = 1.1$ mm, $\rho = 1350$ kg/m³, $v = 0.6$ m/s, $M_i = 30\%$, $M_f = 12.5\%$.

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Die hole radius: $r = 0.55 \times 10^{-3}$ m = $5.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks)** — Cross-section area of one hole:
$$A_{die} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (5.5 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 9.503 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks)** — Mass flow per hole (per second):
$$\dot{m}_{hole} = 1350 \times 0.6 \times 9.503 \times 10^{-7} = 7.697 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 2.771 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks)** — Wet extrudate required (mass balance):
$$\dot{m}_{wet} = 1500 \times \dfrac{(100 - 12.5)}{(100 - 30)} = 1500 \times \dfrac{87.5}{70} = 1875 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks)** — Number of die holes:
$$N_{die} = \dfrac{1875}{2.771} = 676.7 \approx \mathbf{677 \text{ holes}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget to convert moisture to wet-basis for mass balance** — many students apply $(M_i - M_f)$ directly without accounting for the dry-solid conservation rule. Marks lost: ~2.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse $kg/h$ with $kg/s$** in the hole mass-flow calculation. Convert before dividing.
> 3. **Always report rounded values as integers** (e.g., 677 not 676.7) and add a sanity check: industrial spaghetti dies with 1.0–1.2 mm holes use 600–900 holes for ~1 t/h lines — your answer should fall in this range.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 2 Rapid-Fire Revision — Manufacturing of Pasta and Vermicelli**

- ✅ Pasta = **cold extrusion** of durum semolina + water, **< 50 °C**, **0.5–3 MPa**.
- ✅ Vermicelli = thin (0.8–1.2 mm), elongated, often **rice-based or wheat-based**, optionally with tapioca.
- ✅ Raw material: durum wheat semolina (10.5–14 % protein, ash < 0.9 %, lutein pigment).
- ✅ Process chain: **Meter → Mix → Vacuum-Knead → Extrude → Cut → Dry → Cool → Pack**.
- ✅ Vacuum kneading at **−0.6 to −0.8 bar** removes occluded air (prevents white specks and cracks).
- ✅ Die insert options: **bronze (rough, sauce-friendly)** or **PTFE/Teflon (smooth, glossy)**.
- ✅ Die flow is **laminar (Re < 10)** → **Hagen-Poiseuille** applies; throughput ∝ r⁴.
- ✅ Drying reduces moisture from **30 % → 12.5 %** in 3 phases over **18–36 h** (long pasta).
- ✅ Phase 1 = constant rate; Phase 2 = falling rate (most crack-prone); Phase 3 = final equilibration.
- ✅ **"Checkers" = surface cracks** from rapid surface drying; fix by stepwise RH ramp.
- ✅ Drying energy ≈ **3.5–4.5 MJ/kg pasta** (≈ 1 kWh/kg) — biggest energy cost in the plant.
- ✅ Quality: **cooking loss < 7 %**, water absorption **1.6–2.0 g/g**, b* (yellowness) **18–24**.
- ✅ Mass balance equation: $\dot{m}_{wet} = \dot{m}_{product} \times \dfrac{100 - M_f}{100 - M_i}$.
- ✅ Number of die holes: $N_{die} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{product}}{\rho v A_{die}} \times \dfrac{100 - M_f}{100 - M_i}$.
- ✅ Vermicelli: typically **twin-screw**, fine flour, 30–35 % moisture, 4–10 h drying.
- ✅ Global pasta production: **> 17 Mt/yr** (2024); vermicelli: **~3.5 Mt/yr** (Asia-led).
- ✅ Key equipment suppliers for industrial lines: **Bühler (CH), Pavan (IT), Storci (IT)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Twin Screw Extruder: Counter Rotating and Co-rotating Twin Screw Extruder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Twin Screw Extruder: Counter Rotating and Co-rotating Twin Screw Extruder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Twin Screw Extruder (TSE)** is a continuous processing machine that uses **two intermeshing (or non-intermeshing) screws** mounted on parallel shafts within a single barrel, used for compounding, mixing, devolatilization, reactive extrusion, and polymer processing. The two main architectural families are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counter-Rotating Twin Screw Extruder (CRTSE):** Both screws rotate in **opposite directions** (one clockwise, the other counter-clockwise). Predominantly used for **rigid PVC (UPVC) profile and pipe extrusion**. Operates at **low screw speeds (5–50 RPM)** and offers excellent **positive conveying** due to the calendar effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-Rotating Twin Screw Extruder (COTSE):** Both screws rotate in the **same direction** (both clockwise or both counter-clockwise). Operates at **high screw speeds (up to 1200+ RPM)**, features **self-wiping intermeshing geometry**, and is the workhorse for **compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion, and polymer blending**.

| Parameter | Co-Rotating (COTSE) | Counter-Rotating (CRTSE) |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation Direction | Same | Opposite |
| Screw Speed | 100 – 1200 RPM | 5 – 50 RPM |
| Primary Application | Compounding, Blending | PVC Profile, Pipe |
| Self-Wiping | Yes | No |
| Residence Time | Short & Uniform | Longer |
| Material Drag | Forced Drag Flow | Calendar/Pressure Effect |
| Typical Polymer | PE, PP, PS, Nylon, Engineering Resins | Rigid PVC (UPVC/RPVC) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Pestle Mortar" Concept

Imagine a large stone mortar (the **barrel**) where two cooks (the **screws**) grind and push the spice mix (the **polymer melt**) toward the door:

- **Co-rotating** = Both cooks are stirring **in the same circular direction** like two synchronized train wheels. The mix gets "pulled" and "wiped" very efficiently, but it's a high-speed, high-shear operation — perfect for fast gourmet blending (compounding).
- **Counter-rotating** = The cooks are stirring **toward each other** like a clothes wringer. The material gets squeezed between them (the **calendar/nip effect**), creating enormous **pressure** but at a slow, deliberate pace — perfect for gently forcing a stiff dough (PVC) into precise shapes (pipes and profiles).

> [!NOTE]
> The single most important physical phenomenon in CRTSE is the **"Calendar Effect"** (also called the *nip effect* or *converging wedge effect*): as material is dragged by both screws into the tight V-shaped intermeshing zone, it cannot escape back, generating a **positive displacement pressure pulse** with no backflow.

## 1.3 Classification of Twin Screw Extruders

Twin screw extruders are classified by **two orthogonal criteria**:

**A. By Direction of Rotation:**
1. **Co-rotating (Co-TSE)** — intermeshing or non-intermeshing
2. **Counter-rotating (Ctr-TSE)** — intermeshing or non-intermeshing

**B. By Degree of Intermesh:**
1. **Intermeshing** — flight of one screw penetrates the channel of the other
   - *Fully intermeshing* (self-wiping possible in co-rotating)
   - *Partially intermeshing*
2. **Non-intermeshing** — screws do not touch; behaves like two parallel single screws
   - Used for **devolatilization** and **reactive extrusion**

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** specifically emphasizes **intermeshing co-rotating and counter-rotating** machines as they dominate the polymer processing industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Screw Geometry and Key Parameters

The two screws in a TSE share a common **screw diameter D**, and their **centerline distance C** is critical:

- **Centerline distance (C):** Distance between the two screw axes.
- **Inter-shaft clearance:** Gap between screw tips.
- **Flight lead (L):** Axial distance for one full flight turn.
- **Flight depth (H):** Radial depth of the channel.
- **Channel width (W):** Measured perpendicular to flight.

For an **intermeshing** TSE, the geometric requirement is:

$$1.0 \leq \frac{C}{D} \leq 1.05 \quad \text{(non-intermeshing would be } C/D \geq 1.05 \text{)}$$

The **degree of intermesh** $\varepsilon$ is defined as the ratio of the screw-tip penetration depth to the screw radius:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{D - C}{D}$$

> For **fully intermeshing co-rotating**, $\varepsilon \to 1$ (deeply intermeshed), enabling self-wiping.

## 2.2 Flow Mechanisms in Twin Screw Extruders

Three primary flow mechanisms dominate TSE:

1. **Drag Flow (Q_d):** Forward transport due to relative motion of screw and barrel.
2. **Pressure Flow (Q_p):** Backward flow due to die pressure at the discharge.
3. **Leakage Flow (Q_L):** Flow over the flight tips.

For a single screw, the net output is:

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$$

In a TSE, additional **calender (nip) flow** appears in the intermeshing zone for counter-rotating designs.

## 2.3 Co-Rotating Intermeshing Twin Screw Extruder

### 2.3.1 Working Principle
- Both screws rotate in the same direction, e.g., both clockwise.
- Material is conveyed in a **figure-of-eight (lemniscate) shaped C-chamber** that crosses from one screw to the other.
- The flight of one screw *wipes* the root of the other screw → **self-wiping action**.
- This prevents polymer degradation from long residence time.

### 2.3.2 Screw Element Modular Design
The COTSE screw is built from **modular elements** on a common splined shaft:

| Element | Function | Mixing Intensity |
|---|---|---|
| **Conveying (Forward Helix)** | Material transport | Low |
| **Reverse Helix** | Creates back-pressure / mixing zone | Medium |
| **Kneading Discs (KB)** | Dispersive & distributive mixing | High |
| **Tooth Mixer** | Intensive distributive mixing | Very High |
| **Neutral (90°) Kneading** | Pure mixing, no conveying | High |
| **Reverse Kneading** | Backpressure, dispersion | High |

### 2.3.3 Output Equation (Newtonian Approximation)
For a COTSE with **n parallel C-chambers** in the conveying section:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \, \pi^2 \, D^2 \, N \, \sin\theta \cos\theta \, W \cdot n$$

$$Q_p = -\frac{W \cdot H^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \, \mu \, L}$$

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$$

Where:
- $N$ = screw speed (rev/s)
- $\theta$ = helix angle
- $W$ = channel width
- $H$ = channel depth
- $\mu$ = polymer melt viscosity
- $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along the section
- $L$ = axial length

## 2.4 Counter-Rotating Intermeshing Twin Screw Extruder

### 2.4.1 Working Principle — The Calendar Effect
When the two screws rotate in opposite directions, the flights of the two screws **converge** in the intermeshing zone, creating a **V-shaped nip**. Material in this nip is:

1. **Compressed** by the two converging flights
2. **Dragged downward** between the screws
3. **Released** into the next conveying chamber of the opposite screw

The pressure generated in the nip follows the **calendar flow equation** (analogous to a roll nip in calendering):

$$\Delta P_{nip} = \frac{3 \, \mu \, V \, R}{H_{nip}^2}$$

Where:
- $V$ = tangential velocity of screw surface
- $R$ = effective radius
- $H_{nip}$ = minimum clearance in the nip region

### 2.4.2 Output for Counter-Rotating
For CRTSE, the **drag flow component dominates** and pressure flow is largely suppressed due to the closed C-chamber geometry. The output is:

$$Q_{CRTSE} \approx 2 \cdot Q_{single-screw, drag} = \pi^2 \, D^2 \, N \, \sin\theta\cos\theta \, W \cdot n$$

The factor of **2** arises because the material moves in **two** independent C-chambers, one per screw.

### 2.4.3 Why CRTSE for PVC?
- **Rigid PVC is shear- and heat-sensitive** (degrades to HCl above ~180°C).
- Low screw speeds (≤30 RPM) prevent excessive viscous dissipation.
- Excellent **positive displacement** → uniform die pressure → uniform product dimensions.
- The converging nip acts as a **mechanical pressure booster** for highly viscous PVC melt.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_d = 0.5 \, \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta\cos\theta \, W \cdot n$ | Drag flow per screw chamber | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_p = W H^3 \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$ | Pressure back-flow | m³/s |
| 3 | $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$ | Net output of section | m³/s |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{nip} = 3 \mu V R / H_{nip}^2$ | Calendar/pressure in nip (CRTSE) | Pa |
| 5 | $P = 2\pi N T$ | Power consumption (per screw) | W |
| 6 | $SEC = P_{total} / \dot{m}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | kWh/kg |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = (D - C)/D$ | Degree of intermesh | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{res} = V_{free} / Q$ | Mean residence time | s |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$ | Average shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\sigma = \eta_0 \dot{\gamma}$ | Shear stress (Newtonian) | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> In **all KTU examination answers**, always state assumptions: *Newtonian melt*, *fully filled channel*, *steady state*, *isothermal flow*. These earn easy marks.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Masterbatch Manufacturing:** COTSE with kneading discs achieves **dispersive mixing** for pigment agglomerates < 5 μm.
- **Glass-Fiber Compounding:** Reverse kneading elements preserve **fiber length** while achieving distribution.
- **UPVC Pipe Extrusion:** CRTSE with conical screws extrudes at **200–800 kg/h** for 110–400 mm pipe diameters.
- **Reactive Extrusion:** Long L/D (40–60) COTSE enables **in-situ polymerization** and grafting reactions.
- **Devolatilization:** Non-intermeshing COTSE with venting zones removes monomers/solvents at < 1 mbar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drag Flow in a Single C-Chamber of a Co-Rotating TSE

We derive the drag flow component by integrating the velocity profile across the channel depth, using a **Newtonian fluid** assumption and an **unwrapped channel** model.

### Step 1: Geometry Setup
Unwrap the screw channel onto a flat plane. The barrel becomes the top plate moving with velocity:

$$V_b = \pi D N$$

The bottom plate (screw root) is stationary. Channel depth $H$ is measured perpendicular to the barrel surface.

### Step 2: Coordinate System
- $x$ = down-channel direction
- $y$ = depth direction (normal to barrel, from screw root to barrel)
- $z$ = cross-channel direction

### Step 3: Simplifying Assumptions
1. Steady, isothermal, fully developed flow.
2. Pressure varies only in $x$: $P = P(x)$.
3. No flow in $z$-direction.
4. Newtonian fluid: $\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{dv_x}{dy}$.

### Step 4: Navier–Stokes Reduction
The $x$-momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 5: Double Integration
Integrating twice with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial v_x}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y + C_1$$

$$v_x = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions
- At $y = 0$ (screw root): $v_x = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$
- At $y = H$ (barrel wall): $v_x = V_b = \pi D N$

$$\pi D N = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} H^2 + C_1 H$$

$$C_1 = \frac{\pi D N}{H} - \frac{H}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

### Step 7: Velocity Profile

$$v_x(y) = \pi D N \left( \frac{y}{H} \right) - \frac{H^2}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\left[ \frac{y}{H} - \left(\frac{y}{H}\right)^2 \right]$$

### Step 8: Integrate Across Channel to Get Flow Rate
Flow rate per chamber:

$$Q = W \int_0^H v_x \, dy$$

Evaluating the integral term-by-term:

$$\int_0^H \pi D N \frac{y}{H} dy = \frac{\pi D N H}{2}$$

$$\int_0^H \frac{H^2}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\left[\frac{y}{H} - \frac{y^2}{H^2}\right] dy = \frac{H^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

### Step 9: Final Drag Flow Result (per chamber)
Identifying $\partial P / \partial x \cdot L = -\Delta P$:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \pi D N H W = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta\cos\theta \, W$$

We used the geometric identity $H = \frac{D}{2}\sin\theta\cos\theta$ (for standard metric screw profile).

### Step 10: Multi-Chamber Output (COTSE)
For $n$ parallel C-chambers in the conveying section, the total drag flow is:

$$Q_{d,total} = n \cdot \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta\cos\theta \, W$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Calendar (Nip) Pressure in CRTSE

### Step 1: Geometry of the Intermeshing Zone
In the nip region, the two screw flights form a converging wedge. Model the two rotating cylinders as parallel disks (lubrication approximation). The minimum gap is $H_0$, the gap at the entry is $H_1$, and the velocity of each disk is $V = \pi D N$.

### Step 2: Reynolds Equation
For an isoviscous Newtonian fluid, the Reynolds equation in the nip:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left( H^3 \frac{dP}{dx} \right) = 6 \mu V \frac{dH}{dx}$$

### Step 3: Gap Function
Linear converging gap:

$$H(x) = H_0 + (H_1 - H_0)\left(1 - \frac{x}{L_{nip}}\right)$$

Let $H_1 - H_0 = \Delta H$. The slope is constant:

$$\frac{dH}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta H}{L_{nip}} = \text{const}$$

### Step 4: Integrate
$$\int \frac{d}{dx}\left(H^3 \frac{dP}{dx}\right) dx = 6 \mu V \int \frac{dH}{dx} dx$$

$$H^3 \frac{dP}{dx} = 6 \mu V H + C$$

### Step 5: Boundary Conditions
At the nip entry ($x = -L_{nip}/2$, $H = H_1$), $P = P_0$ (atmospheric).
At the nip exit ($x = +L_{nip}/2$, $H = H_0$), symmetry gives $dP/dx = 0$ → $C = -6 \mu V H_0$.

### Step 6: Maximum Pressure (Reynolds Boundary Condition)
Solving for the maximum pressure at the nip exit:

$$P_{max} = P_0 + \frac{3 \mu V}{H_0}\left[\frac{(H_1 - H_0)^2}{2 H_0^2}\right]$$

This is the **calendar pressure boost** characteristic of CRTSE — it is what gives counter-rotating extruders their exceptional **pumping efficiency at low speeds**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Co-Rotating (COTSE) | Counter-Rotating (CRTSE) |
|---|---|---|
| Screw Geometry | Parallel, identical | Parallel, mirror-image (often conical) |
| Channel Shape | Figure-8 (lemniscate) | Independent C-chambers |
| Material Transfer | Cross-flow between screws | No cross-flow |
| Self-Wiping | Yes (eliminates stagnant zones) | No (material may stagnate in nip) |
| Pressure Capability | Moderate (with restrictive elements) | Very High (calendar effect) |
| Shear Heating | Distributive | Concentrated in nip |
| Throughput | High | Moderate |
| Flexibility | High (modular screws) | Low (fixed geometry typical) |
| Maintenance Cost | Moderate (modular element inventory) | High (precision matching required) |
| Typical L/D | 24 – 60 | 16 – 24 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between co-rotating and counter-rotating twin screw extruders with respect to screw speed, mixing action, and typical applications. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Co-Rotating | Counter-Rotating |
|---|---|---|
| **Screw Speed** | High (100–1200 RPM) | Low (5–50 RPM) |
| **Mixing Action** | Self-wiping; figure-8 chamber; distributive + dispersive | No self-wiping; calendar/nip mixing in V-zone |
| **Typical Application** | Compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion | UPVC pipes, profiles, sheets |

The co-rotating design enables **modular screw elements** (kneading discs, tooth mixers) for flexible configuration, while the counter-rotating design provides **inherent positive displacement** from the converging nip. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is meant by the "calendar effect" in a counter-rotating twin screw extruder? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **calendar effect** (also called the **nip effect**) refers to the localized pressure generation in the **V-shaped intermeshing zone** where the flights of the two counter-rotating screws converge. As material is dragged into this narrow wedge, it cannot escape, and a strong **Reynolds-type pressure pulse** is developed according to:

$$\Delta P_{nip} = \frac{3 \mu V R}{H_0^2}$$

This pressure boost is what enables CRTSE to pump highly viscous materials like rigid PVC at very low screw speeds. **(3 Marks)**

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Module Pattern: Internal Choice provided. Answer ANY ONE of the two sub-choices below.]**

---

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]`
**(a) With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of a counter-rotating intermeshing twin screw extruder used for rigid PVC pipe extrusion. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the calendar pressure equation for the nip zone and explain its significance in determining the throughput of a counter-rotating TSE. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

A **counter-rotating intermeshing TSE** consists of two screws, often **parallel and identical** (or **conical** in newer designs), mounted in a figure-8 shaped barrel. Key components:

1. **Barrel (C-Shaped Cross-Section):** Made of bimetallic tool steel; often jacketed for thermal oil or water circulation. The barrel is segmented to allow heating/cooling zones.
2. **Screws (Conical or Parallel):** Designed as **mirror images** of each other. Conical screws provide higher torque transmission and better feeding.
3. **Drive System:** Geared motor with **synchronizing gears** that ensure the screws rotate in opposite directions at the same speed.
4. **Feed Throat:** Cooled to prevent PVC powder from sticking (PVC is sticky at ~80°C).
5. **Vacuum Venting Port:** For removal of HCl traces and entrapped air.
6. **Die Adapter:** Connects the extruder to the profile/pipe die.

**Working Principle for UPVC Pipe:**
- PVC powder + stabilizer/lubricant blend is fed via a gravimetric feeder.
- In the **feed zone**, the material is cold-compacted by the counter-rotating flight action.
- In the **plasticating zone**, heat from external heaters and viscous dissipation raises the temperature to **170–185°C** (controlled tightly to prevent PVC degradation).
- The **calendar effect** in the intermeshing nip generates the high pressure needed to force the melt through the die.
- The output pipe enters a **calibration/sizing sleeve** followed by a **spray cooling tank** and is hauled off and cut.

**(b) Calendar Pressure Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes. The pressure at the nip is:

$$P_{max} = P_0 + \frac{3 \mu V}{H_0}\left[\frac{(H_1 - H_0)^2}{2 H_0^2}\right]$$

Step-by-step:
1. Model nip as two converging parallel disks → lubrication approximation valid.
2. Apply Reynolds equation for isoviscous flow.
3. Integrate with $H(x)$ linear, $dP/dx = 0$ at the nip exit.
4. Boundary conditions: $P = P_0$ at nip entry, symmetry at exit.

**Significance:**
- Determines **maximum pressure** the extruder can generate without die-plate starvation.
- $P_{max} \propto \mu$ → highly viscous polymers (PVC) get larger pressure boosts.
- $P_{max} \propto 1/H_0^3$ → clearance is the most sensitive design parameter.
- $P_{max} \propto V \propto N$ → throughput scales linearly with screw speed (Newtonian).

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]`
**(a) Explain the modular screw element design of a co-rotating twin screw extruder. List and describe the functions of FOUR different screw elements. (7 Marks)**

**(b) Derive the drag flow equation for a single C-chamber of a co-rotating TSE and explain how the multi-chamber geometry affects net output. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Modular Screw Element Design (7 Marks):**

The **COTSE screw** is built by assembling discrete elements onto a **common splined shaft**. The elements can be reconfigured for any process in minutes. Four key elements:

1. **Conveying Element (Forward Helix, Lead ≈ 1.0D):** Standard conveying flight; provides positive forward drag flow. **Function:** Transport material along the screw.
2. **Kneading Disc Element (typically 30°/45°/60°/90° stagger):** Staggered arrangement of discs creates cross-channel flow. **Function:** Dispersive and distributive mixing; the stagger angle controls net conveying vs. mixing.
3. **Reverse Helix Element (Lead = -0.5D to -1.0D):** Flights angled oppositely. **Function:** Creates a sealed, pressurized zone — used to force melting, build die pressure, or act as a "dynamic seal" before vents.
4. **Tooth Mixer Element (SME — Smith-type):** Semicircular teeth arranged around the screw. **Function:** High-intensity distributive mixing with minimal residence time, used for color dispersion and additive incorporation.

**Modularity benefit:** Same extruder can be reconfigured for **glass-fiber compounding** (reverse kneading) vs. **color masterbatch** (tooth mixers) vs. **reactive extrusion** (long reverse sealing elements).

**(b) Drag Flow Derivation (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final drag flow per C-chamber is:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta \, W$$

Step-by-step:
1. Unwrap the channel → flat geometry.
2. Navier–Stokes reduces to $\partial^2 v_x / \partial y^2 = (1/\mu)\partial P/\partial x$.
3. Apply no-slip BCs: $v_x(0) = 0$ and $v_x(H) = \pi D N$.
4. Integrate to get velocity profile, then integrate across $H$ to get $Q_d$.

**Multi-Chamber Effect:**
- In a COTSE, the **C-chamber is split into two halves** by the intermeshing flight.
- Each screw has $n$ parallel C-chambers, and material crosses from one screw to the other in the intermeshing region.
- **Net output** = $n \times Q_{d,single}$ because the chambers act in parallel.
- This is why COTSE achieves throughputs of **500–10,000 kg/h** at high speeds, whereas a single screw of the same diameter would be limited to ~$Q_d/2$ at the same $N$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the L/D ratio direction:** Co-rotating extruders use $L/D$ measured along *one screw* (e.g., $L = 40D$), not the barrel length. Mention this explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the centerline distance constraint:** $C/D$ must be **1.0–1.05 for intermeshing**. Drawing screws too far apart is a common diagram error.
> 3. **Mixing up "self-wiping" and "self-feeding":** Self-wiping is a property of **co-rotating**; self-feeding/positive conveying is a property of **counter-rotating**.
> 4. **Not specifying Newtonian assumptions:** Always declare "Newtonian melt" before applying $Q_d$ and $Q_p$ formulas; otherwise the examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for CRTSE output:** $Q_{CRTSE} = 2 \times Q_{single}$ because there are two independent C-chambers (one per screw).

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Remember Points for the KTU Board Exam:**

- **Twin Screw Extruder (TSE):** Two screws in one barrel, classified by **rotation direction** and **degree of intermesh**.
- **Co-Rotating (COTSE):** Same rotation direction; **high speed (100–1200 RPM)**; **self-wiping**; **modular screw elements**; primary use is **compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion**.
- **Counter-Rotating (CRTSE):** Opposite rotation direction; **low speed (5–50 RPM)**; **calendar effect in nip**; primary use is **UPVC pipe and profile extrusion**.
- **C-Chamber (COTSE):** Figure-8 lemniscate-shaped chamber formed by the intermeshing of two screws. Material crosses from one screw to the other in each cycle.
- **Calendar / Nip Effect (CRTSE):** Pressure generated in the V-shaped intermeshing zone; $\Delta P_{nip} = 3\mu V R / H_0^2$. Enables positive displacement pumping at low speeds.
- **Drag Flow Equation (Per C-Chamber):** $Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N \sin\theta \cos\theta \, W$. Multiplied by $n$ for total output.
- **Pressure Flow:** $Q_p = W H^3 \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$ — acts in the reverse direction of drag flow.
- **Modular Elements (COTSE):** Forward conveying, kneading discs (30°/45°/60°/90°), reverse helix, tooth mixers.
- **Degree of Intermesh:** $\varepsilon = (D - C)/D$. For fully intermeshing, $\varepsilon \to 1$.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** $SEC = P / \dot{m}$, expressed in **kWh/kg**. Typical COTSE: 0.1–0.4 kWh/kg.
- **Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma} = \pi D N / H$ — important for thermal stability of PVC.
- **L/D Ratio:** COTSE: 24–60; CRTSE: 16–24.
- **Centerline Distance Ratio:** $C/D \leq 1.05$ for intermeshing, $\geq 1.05$ for non-intermeshing.
- **Self-Wiping** = property of co-rotating only; prevents polymer stagnation.
- **Conical Screws** are increasingly used in modern CRTSE for UPVC for higher torque and better feeding.
- **Process Windows:**
  - **COTSE:** $T_{melt}$ varies by polymer (PE ~200–230 °C, Nylon ~270–290 °C).
  - **CRTSE (UPVC):** $T_{melt} = 170–185 °C$ (strict — degradation above 195 °C).
- **Industrial Trivia:** Conical counter-rotating TSEs for UPVC pipe were popularized by **Krauss-Maffei** (now part of various OEMs) and **Cincinnati Milacron**; modern COTSE compounders are dominated by Coperion, Leistritz, and Thermo Scientific.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — TOPIC: Twin Screw Extruder: Counter Rotating and Co-rotating Twin Screw Extruder**
> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4), and Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Course Outcomes CO1–CO3 of PEFTT637 — Advanced Extrusion Technology.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rheological Properties of Materials During the Extrusion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rheological Properties of Materials During the Extrusion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Rheology in the Context of Extrusion?

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheology** is the branch of physics/science that deals with the **deformation and flow of matter**, particularly the flow behavior of materials that are neither pure elastic solids nor simple Newtonian fluids. In extrusion technology, rheology is the *governing science* that determines how a polymer melt, food dough, metal billet, or ceramic paste moves through the barrel, screw channels, and die geometry.

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

Rheological properties of materials during extrusion describe the **time-dependent and rate-dependent response** of a material under the imposed **shear stresses and shear strains** generated by the rotating screw, the barrel wall, and the converging die land. For the KTU 2024 scheme, rheology encompasses:

- **Viscosity (η)** — internal resistance to flow, expressed in **Pa·s**.
- **Shear stress (τ)** — force per unit area acting tangentially, expressed in **Pa** or **N/m²**.
- **Shear rate (γ̇ or $\dot{\gamma}$)** — velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction, expressed in **s⁻¹**.
- **Flow behavior index (n)** — dimensionless parameter indicating deviation from Newtonian behavior.
- **Consistency index (K)** — measure of material stiffness, expressed in **Pa·sⁿ**.
- **Yield stress (τ₀)** — minimum stress required to initiate flow, expressed in **Pa**.
- **Elastic modulus (G′) and viscous modulus (G″)** — for viscoelastic characterization.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Making Rheology Click

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Toothpaste Tube Analogy:** Imagine you squeeze a tube of toothpaste. The paste does **not** splatter out like water. It flows slowly, holds its shape on the brush, and resists fast splashing. Now try the same experiment with honey, ketchup, and water. Each behaves **completely differently** under identical squeezing. This *flow personality* of each material is its **rheological fingerprint**. Extrusion engineers must understand this fingerprint because the screw-and-die system is essentially a *very sophisticated, high-pressure version of the toothpaste tube.*

### Real-World Rheological Comparison

| Material | Rheological Class | Behavior Under Squeeze |
|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Water | Newtonian | Flows instantly, splashes freely |
| Honey | Newtonian (high viscosity) | Flows slowly but proportionally |
| Toothpaste | Bingham / Herschel-Bulkley | Holds shape until threshold stress |
| Ketchup | Shear-thinning (Pseudoplastic) | Becomes runnier when shaken |
| Cornstarch + water | Shear-thickening (Dilatant) | Hardens when struck hard |
| Polymer melt (LDPE) | Power-law (Pseudoplastic) | Viscosity drops as shear rate rises |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** In extrusion, **> 95% of polymers, food materials, and ceramic pastes are NON-NEWTONIAN**. Treating them as Newtonian fluids is the single most common mistake students make in KTU board examinations.

---

## 1.3 Why Rheology Matters in Extrusion

The extruder screw generates shear rates ranging from **10 s⁻¹** (in the solids conveying zone) to **> 10,000 s⁻¹** (in the die land region). Across this enormous range, a polymer's viscosity may drop by **2–3 orders of magnitude**. Without proper rheological understanding:

- The die swell (extrudate swell) cannot be predicted.
- Melt fracture (sharkskin) defects cannot be explained.
- Pressure-flow relationships in the die land become inaccurate.
- Power consumption calculations are wrong.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Materials by Rheological Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Master Rheological Classes for KTU 2024:**

1. **Newtonian Fluids** — τ = η·γ̇ (constant viscosity). Examples: water, glycerol, silicone oils, molten metals at low strain.
2. **Pseudoplastic (Shear-Thinning)** — viscosity *decreases* with shear rate. Examples: most polymer melts (PE, PP, PS, PVC), blood, paint.
3. **Dilatant (Shear-Thickening)** — viscosity *increases* with shear rate. Examples: cornstarch suspensions, high-solid ceramic pastes, wet sand.
4. **Bingham Plastics** — behave as a solid until yield stress τ₀ is exceeded, then flow linearly. Examples: toothpaste, drilling mud, chocolate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constitutive Equations — The Mathematical Heart of Rheology

A **constitutive equation** is a mathematical relationship that links stress to deformation history. The KTU 2024 scheme requires mastery of **five constitutive models**. Below is the exhaustive KTU Formula Sheet.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — These equations appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on rheology.**

| # | Model Name | Constitutive Equation | Apparent Viscosity η(γ̇) | Key Parameters | Typical Material |
|---|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Newtonian** | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta = \text{constant}$ | η (Pa·s) | Water, glycerol |
| 2 | **Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele)** | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\eta_{app} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | K (Pa·sⁿ), n (—) | Most polymer melts |
| 3 | **Bingham Plastic** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + \eta_{p} \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_{0}}{\dot{\gamma}} + \eta_{p}$ | τ₀ (Pa), ηₚ (Pa·s) | Toothpaste, mud |
| 4 | **Herschel-Bulkley** | $\tau = \tau_{0} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ | $\eta_{app} = \dfrac{\tau_{0}}{\dot{\gamma}} + K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | τ₀, K, n | Chocolate, cement |
| 5 | **Carreau** | $\dfrac{\eta - \eta_{\infty}}{\eta_{0} - \eta_{\infty}} = \left[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^{2}\right]^{\frac{n-1}{2}}$ | $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ | η₀, η∞, λ, n | Broad-shear-range polymer melts |

---

## 2.3 The Power-Law Fluid — KTU's Most Tested Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Power-Law dominates KTU questions:** It is mathematically tractable, captures the essential non-linear behavior of most polymers over 2–3 decades of shear rate, and integrates cleanly into the **Rabinowitsch correction** for non-Newtonian flow in dies.

### Power-Law Parameters — Physical Meaning

- **Consistency Index K**: A larger K means a *stiffer*, more viscous material. K is highly temperature-dependent. Typical values for LDPE at 200 °C: **K ≈ 2.5 × 10⁴ Pa·sⁿ**.
- **Flow Behavior Index n**: 
  - n = 1 → Newtonian (K becomes η)
  - n < 1 → Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning) — typical polymer melts: **n = 0.2 to 0.6**
  - n > 1 → Dilatant (shear-thickening) — typical concentrated suspensions: **n = 1.1 to 1.5**

### Temperature Dependence (Arrhenius-type)

$$ \eta(T) = \eta_{0} \cdot \exp\left[\frac{E_{a}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{0}}\right)\right] $$

where:
- $E_{a}$ = activation energy of flow (J/mol), typically **20,000–80,000 J/mol** for polyolefins.
- R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** — universal gas constant.
- T = absolute temperature (K).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A 10 °C rise in melt temperature typically reduces viscosity by ~30–50%. This is why **temperature control is the single most powerful viscosity-control knob** in industrial extrusion.

---

## 2.4 Wall Shear Stress & Wall Shear Rate in Dies

For a cylindrical die (capillary rheometer geometry) of radius **R** and length **L** with pressure drop **ΔP**:

$$ \tau_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2L} $$

$$ \dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} $$

where Q = volumetric flow rate (m³/s).

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Rheology in Real Extrusion

| Engineering Decision | Rheological Property Used |
|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Screw speed selection | Shear-rate dependent η |
| Die land length design | Wall shear stress (to avoid melt fracture) |
| Barrel temperature profile | Arrhenius temperature dependence |
| Pressure-flow relationship | Power-law model + Rabinowitsch |
