The **Factors Affecting Extrusion** are the independent variables (input parameters) of the extrusion process that govern the dependent variables such as extrusion pressure (ram pressure), product quality (surface finish, dimensional accuracy), die life, and the upper limit of the extrusion ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> The performance, feasibility, and economics of any extrusion operation are governed by **six inter-dependent categories** of factors: (i) Material factors, (ii) Process/Thermomechanical factors, (iii) Tool and Die factors, (iv) Friction and Lubrication factors, (v) Product/Geometric factors, and (vi) Equipment/Machine factors. A change in any single factor cascades through the entire process window.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you have a **toothpaste tube**. When you press the tube from the back, the thick paste is forced to flow through the narrow nozzle at the front and emerges as a thin, continuous ribbon. The *amount of force* you need depends on six things you can intuitively control:

- **How thick the paste is** → (Material property → Flow stress, % reduction in area)
- **How cold the paste is** → (Process temperature → Cold, Warm, or Hot extrusion)
- **How fast you push** → (Ram speed → Strain rate sensitivity)
- **How rough the tube walls are inside** → (Friction between billet and container)
- **The shape and length of the nozzle** → (Die angle, die land length)
- **How big the tube is vs. how big the nozzle is** → (Extrusion ratio, R = A₀/A₁)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember (KTU High-Yield):**
> - Coefficient of friction in hot extrusion: $\mu = 0.05 - 0.25$
> - Coefficient of friction in cold extrusion: $\mu = 0.05 - 0.10$ (with lubrication)
> - Typical hot extrusion temperatures for Aluminum: **400 – 500 °C**
> - Typical hot extrusion temperatures for Copper: **700 – 900 °C**
> - Typical hot extrusion temperatures for Steel: **1100 – 1300 °C**
> - Maximum theoretical extrusion ratio (without billet-to-container friction): $R_{max} \approx e^2 \approx 7.39$

### 1.3 Engineering Significance

In real industrial practice (e.g., aluminum section plants in Kerala such as INDAL/HEROcycles supply chain, or copper wire drawing industries), selecting the correct combination of factors determines whether the process is:
- **Feasible** (can the press deliver the required tonnage?)
- **Economical** (is die life and energy consumption acceptable?)
- **Quality-compliant** (surface finish, dimensional tolerance, structural integrity)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** Use the acronym **"M-P-T-F-P-E"** → **M**aterial, **P**rocess (thermal/mechanical), **T**ool/Die, **F**riction, **P**roduct Geometry, **E**quipment.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Categories of Factors – Structured Analysis

#### **A. Material Factors**

| Sub-Factor | Effect on Extrusion |
|---|---|
| **Flow Stress (σ₀ or K)** | Higher $\sigma_0$ → Higher extrusion pressure. Directly proportional. |
| **Strain Hardening Exponent (n)** | Higher *n* (e.g., annealed metals) → More uniform flow, lower pressure. |
| **Strain Rate Sensitivity (m)** | Higher *m* (e.g., hot metals) → Delays necking, allows higher speeds. |
| **Initial Temperature of Billet** | Higher $T_0$ → Lower $\sigma_0$ (due to thermal softening) → Lower pressure. |
| **Alloying Composition** | Alloys (e.g., Al-Cu 2014) are stronger and have higher extrusion pressure than pure metals (e.g., Al 1100). |
| **Microstructure (Grain size)** | Finer grains → Higher strength → Higher pressure, better surface finish. |

#### **B. Process / Thermomechanical Factors**

- **Extrusion Temperature (T):** Cold (< 0.3 $T_m$), Warm (0.3–0.5 $T_m$), Hot (> 0.5 $T_m$).
- **Ram Speed / Strain Rate ($\dot{\varepsilon}$):** Higher $\dot{\varepsilon}$ → Higher pressure (hot work) due to adiabatic heating.
- **Extrusion Ratio (R):** $R = A_0 / A_f$ where $A_0$ = billet area, $A_f$ = product area. Higher *R* → Higher pressure.
- **Reduction in Area (%RA):** $\% RA = (1 - 1/R) \times 100\%$

#### **C. Tool and Die Factors**

- **Die Angle (2α):** Affects dead metal zone (DMZ) shape. Optimal angle minimizes pressure.
- **Die Land Length (L_d):** Provides shape control but adds frictional resistance.
- **Die Material & Hardness:** Determines die life, especially in hot extrusion (thermal fatigue).
- **Bearing Length:** Critical for maintaining dimensional accuracy of the product.

#### **D. Friction and Lubrication Factors**

- **Billet-to-Container Friction ($\mu_c$):** Adds redundant work. Major contributor to pressure.
- **Billet-to-Die Friction ($\mu_d$):** Affects surface quality of the product.
- **Lubricant Type:** Glass-based (hot extrusion of steel, copper), oil/graphite (cold), phosphate soap (cold steel).

#### **E. Product / Geometric Factors**

- **Complexity of Cross-Section:** Hollow sections (porthole dies) require higher pressure.
- **Wall Thickness Uniformity:** Affects metal flow balance.
- **Product Length:** Determines if a direct or indirect process is preferred.

#### **F. Equipment / Machine Factors**

- **Press Capacity (Tonnage):** Must exceed calculated extrusion pressure × safety factor (1.3–1.5).
- **Container Diameter vs. Length (D/L ratio):** Affects friction and buckling.
- **Stiffness of Press Frame:** Influences product straightness (deflection).
- **Speed Control:** Modern hydraulic presses allow variable speed profiles.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** In the table below, all mathematical "absolute value" or "magnitude" notations use `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar command) — never a raw `|` — to keep markdown table syntax intact.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ideal Homogeneous Extrusion Pressure** (no friction) | $P_{ideal} = \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)$ | $P_{ideal}$ in MPa, $\sigma_0$ in MPa, $R$ = ratio | Theoretical minimum pressure for homogeneous deformation. |
| 2 | **Actual Direct Extrusion Pressure (with friction)** | $P = \sigma_0 \ln(R) + \frac{4 \mu L_b}{D_b} \cdot \sigma_0$ | $L_b$ = billet length, $D_b$ = billet diameter, $\mu$ = coeff. of friction | Used for ALL direct (forward) extrusion calculations. |
| 3 | **Extrusion Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$ | $A_0$ = billet area, $A_f$ = product area (mm²) | Quantifies severity of deformation. |
| 4 | **Reduction in Area (RA)** | $\% RA = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{R}\right) \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Industry standard for reporting severity. |
| 5 | **True Strain in Extrusion** | $\varepsilon = \ln(R)$ | Dimensionless | Equivalent to true strain in the deformation zone. |
| 6 | **Indirect (Backward) Extrusion Pressure** | $P_{indirect} = \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)$ | Same as Eq. 1, no friction term | Friction is eliminated; only deformation work. |
| 7 | **Temperature Rise (Adiabatic Heating)** | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)}{\rho \cdot C_p}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat | Predicts hot spots that may cause tearing. |
| 8 | **Flow Stress with Temperature Compensation** | $\sigma_0 = K \cdot \varepsilon^n \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}^m \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ | $Q$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant | Hot extrusion pressure prediction. |
| 9 | **Maximum Possible Extrusion Ratio** | $R_{max} = e^{\left(\dfrac{P_{press}}{\sigma_0}\right)}$ | $P_{press}$ = press capacity | Design constraint check. |
| 10 | **Optimum Die Semi-Angle (Cone die)** | $\alpha_{opt} \approx 45° - 60°$ (varies with $\mu$) | Degrees | Minimizes dead zone + deformation work. |
| 11 | **Ram Pressure for Sticking Friction** | $P_{stick} = \sigma_0 \left[ \ln(R) + \dfrac{2L_b}{D_b} \right]$ | MPa | Worst-case hot extrusion scenario. |
| 12 | **Force on Ram (F)** | $F = P \cdot A_{ram}$ | $F$ in N, $A_{ram}$ in mm² | Press tonnage selection. |

### 2.3 Why These Factors Matter in Real Engineering

- **Material factors** dictate the alloy families a manufacturer can process on a given press.
- **Process factors** control energy efficiency: a 50 °C drop in billet temperature may require **~15–20% more tonnage**.
- **Tool factors** determine **die life**, which is the single largest recurring cost in aluminum extrusion.
- **Friction factors** are addressed through **proper lubrication** — using graphite or glass can cut pressure requirements by 30–40%.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** The student must be able to draw and label a graph of "Extrusion Pressure vs. Stroke" for both direct and indirect extrusion, identifying the **breakthrough pressure peak** and the **steady-state plateau** — these are the two pressure regions sensitive to the factors above.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Homogeneous Extrusion Pressure (P = σ₀ · ln R)

This is the **foundation equation** for all extrusion analysis in KTU Module 4.

**Starting Premise:** Consider a frictionless, homogeneous direct extrusion through a conical die. We apply the slab method (free-body equilibrium of an elemental slice).

**Step 1 – Geometry Setup:**
Let billet cross-section = $A_0$ at position $x$, and deformed cross-section at die exit = $A_f$.
Extrusion ratio: $R = A_0 / A_f$.

**Step 2 – Elemental Slab Equilibrium:**
Consider a small element of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the ram. The element has:
- Force from ram side: $(P + dP) \cdot A_0$
- Force from die side: $P \cdot A_0$
- Internal deformation resistance: $\sigma_0 \cdot A_0$ (acting opposite to flow direction)

**Step 3 – Force Balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x &= 0 \\
(P + dP) \cdot A_0 - P \cdot A_0 - \sigma_0 \cdot A_0 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Simplification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dP \cdot A_0 &= \sigma_0 \cdot A_0 \\
dP &= \sigma_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Integration with Geometric Constraint:**
Since the material conserves volume, $A \cdot dL = \text{constant}$, and as material moves from $A_0$ to $A_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dL &= -\dfrac{L_b}{A_0} \cdot dA \\
\text{Substituting into the strain definition:} \quad d\varepsilon &= -\dfrac{dA}{A} \\
\text{Integrating from } A_0 \text{ to } A_f: \quad \varepsilon &= \ln\left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right) = \ln(R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Final Integration for Pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^P dP &= \sigma_0 \int_0^{\ln R} d\varepsilon \\
\boxed{P_{ideal} = \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this is "ideal":** It assumes no friction and no redundant deformation. In a real KTU numerical problem, you must add the friction term $\dfrac{4\mu L_b}{D_b} \sigma_0$ to obtain the **actual** extrusion pressure.

### 3.2 Derivation of Direct Extrusion Pressure with Container Friction

Starting from the same slab equilibrium, but now including the **frictional shear stress** acting on the container wall:

**Step 1 – Shear Stress on Wall:**
The billet sticks to the container wall under sticking friction:
$\tau = \mu \cdot p_{wall}$ where $p_{wall}$ is the normal pressure.

**Step 2 – Modified Equilibrium (FBD of element):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P + dP) \cdot A_0 - P \cdot A_0 - \sigma_0 \cdot A_0 - \tau \cdot \pi D_b \cdot dx &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Substitute Geometry and Simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dP \cdot A_0 &= \sigma_0 \cdot A_0 \cdot dx + \mu \cdot p_{wall} \cdot \pi D_b \cdot dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Using $A_0 = \pi D_b^2/4$ and $p_{wall} \approx \sigma_0$ (approximation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dP \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4} &= \sigma_0 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_b^2}{4} \cdot dx + \mu \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \pi D_b \cdot dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Divide through by $A_0$ and rearrange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dP &= \sigma_0 \, dx + \dfrac{4 \mu \sigma_0}{D_b} \, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Integrate over the billet length $L_b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^P dP &= \sigma_0 \int_0^{L_b} \dfrac{dx}{1} + \dfrac{4\mu\sigma_0}{D_b} \int_0^{L_b} dx \\
\boxed{P_{direct} = \sigma_0 \ln(R) + \dfrac{4\mu L_b}{D_b} \sigma_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This two-term equation is the most-tested formula in KTU Module 4.** First term = ideal deformation work. Second term = friction work against container walls.

### 3.3 Derivation of Temperature Rise (Adiabatic Heating)

**Step 1 – Energy Balance:**
Plastic deformation converts mechanical work almost entirely into heat (90–95%, hence $\eta \approx 0.9$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{plastic} &= \eta \cdot P \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b \\
\text{Heat generated: } Q &= W_{plastic} = \eta \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R) \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Heat Absorbed by Billet:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Equate and Solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T &= \eta \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R) \cdot A_0 \cdot L_b \\
\boxed{\Delta T = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)}{\rho \cdot C_p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Effect Direction of Each Factor

| Factor | Increase → Effect on Pressure | Increase → Effect on Quality | Increase → Effect on Die Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow Stress ($\sigma_0$) | **↑ Increases** | Neutral (if not too high) | **↓ Reduces** |
| Extrusion Ratio (R) | **↑ Increases (logarithmically)** | Risk of cracking | **↓ Reduces** |
| Billet Length (L_b) | **↑ Increases (friction term)** | Neutral | Neutral |
| Coefficient of Friction ($\mu$) | **↑ Increases** | **↓ Worse surface** | **↓ Reduces** |
| Billet Temperature (T) | **↓ Decreases** | **↑ Better** (in hot ext.) | **↓ Reduces** (thermal fatigue) |
| Ram Speed | **↑ Increases (hot)** | Risk of central burst | Neutral |
| Die Angle (2α) | Minimum at $\alpha_{opt}$ | **↑ Better flow** | Depends on geometry |
| Die Land Length (L_d) | **↑ Slightly increases** | **↑ Better dimensional control** | **↓ Reduces** (wear) |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Extrusion Ratio"** and state its significance in determining the extrusion pressure.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The extrusion ratio **R** is defined as the ratio of the original cross-sectional area of the billet ($A_0$) to the cross-sectional area of the extruded product ($A_f$):

$$R = \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$$

It is a dimensionless number that quantifies the *severity* of plastic deformation. Its significance is twofold: (1) it appears logarithmically in the extrusion pressure equation ($P = \sigma_0 \ln R$), so even a small increase in *R* significantly raises the required tonnage, and (2) it determines whether the process is physically feasible on a given press, since the maximum possible *R* is constrained by press capacity: $R_{max} = \exp(P_{press}/\sigma_0)$. **(3 Marks – full credit if both definition and significance are provided with formula.)**

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2.** List **any four process parameters** that affect the extrusion pressure and briefly explain the role of **die semi-angle** among them.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Four process parameters that affect extrusion pressure are:
1. Extrusion temperature
2. Ram speed / strain rate
3. Extrusion ratio (*R*)
4. Coefficient of friction at the billet-container interface

**Role of die semi-angle (α):** The die semi-angle controls the geometry of the deformation zone and the size of the **dead metal zone (DMZ)**. At very small α, redundant shear work inside the DMZ increases pressure. At very large α, friction along the die-billet interface also increases pressure. An **optimum die angle** (typically 45°–60°) minimizes the total pressure requirement by balancing these two effects. **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **homogeneous extrusion pressure** in a direct extrusion process starting from a slab-method force balance on an elemental disc. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical aluminum billet of diameter **75 mm** and length **200 mm** is extruded at 450 °C into a solid round bar of diameter **25 mm**. The average flow stress of aluminum at this temperature is **60 MPa**, and the coefficient of friction between the billet and the container is **0.10**. Calculate:
  (i) The extrusion ratio *R*
  (ii) The ideal (frictionless) extrusion pressure
  (iii) The actual extrusion pressure including friction
  (iv) The ram force in kN

  Assume sticking friction conditions. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Homogeneous deformation (no redundant work).
2. Frictionless initially (ideal case).
3. Material is isotropic and incompressible.
4. The flow stress $\sigma_0$ is constant throughout deformation.

**Derivation Steps:**

Step 1 — Consider a thin elemental disc of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the ram inside the container. Let the pressure on its left face be $P$ and on its right face be $P + dP$.

Step 2 — The internal resistance force offered by the material to deformation is $\sigma_0 \cdot A_0$, where $A_0$ is the billet cross-sectional area (constant in the container).

Step 3 — Apply Newton's equilibrium in the *x*-direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P + dP) \cdot A_0 - P \cdot A_0 - \sigma_0 \cdot A_0 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Simplify to obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
dP &= \sigma_0
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Integrate both sides. The lower limit of pressure is 0 (at the entry of the deformation zone, ignoring redundant work), and the upper limit is $P_{ideal}$. The strain accumulated is $\varepsilon = \ln(A_0/A_f) = \ln(R)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{P_{ideal}} dP &= \sigma_0 \int_0^{\ln R} d\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 6 — Final result:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{P_{ideal} = \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This expression represents the **minimum possible extrusion pressure** under ideal, frictionless conditions. In real practice, this value is increased by the **billet-container friction** term and **die-entry redundant work** term. **(Full 7 marks if all 6 steps are shown and assumptions listed.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Billet diameter $D_b = 75$ mm, length $L_b = 200$ mm
- Product diameter $d_f = 25$ mm
- Flow stress $\sigma_0 = 60$ MPa
- Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.10$

**(i) Extrusion Ratio R (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{(\pi/4)(75)^2}{(\pi/4)(25)^2} = \left(\dfrac{75}{25}\right)^2 = 3^2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Ideal (Frictionless) Pressure (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ideal} &= \sigma_0 \cdot \ln(R) = 60 \times \ln(9) = 60 \times 2.1972 = 131.83 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Actual Pressure with Friction (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{actual} &= \sigma_0 \ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L_b}{D_b} \sigma_0 \\
P_{actual} &= 60 \times 2.1972 + \dfrac{4 \times 0.10 \times 200}{75} \times 60 \\
P_{actual} &= 131.83 + \dfrac{80}{75} \times 60 = 131.83 + 64.00 = \textbf{195.83 MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Ram Force in kN (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P_{actual} \times A_{ram} = 195.83 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} (75)^2 \\
F &= 195.83 \times 4417.86 = 865,225 \text{ N} \approx \textbf{865.22 kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With the help of a **neat labeled sketch**, explain the **direct (forward) and indirect (backward) extrusion processes**. Compare their **pressure requirements** with appropriate equations and discuss **at least four factors** that influence the extrusion pressure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hot extrusion of a copper billet is performed with the following data:
  - Initial diameter = 100 mm, length = 300 mm
  - Final product diameter = 20 mm
  - Flow stress at extrusion temperature = 35 MPa
  - Coefficient of friction = 0.12
  - Container temperature = 850 °C
  
  Compute:
  (i) The reduction in area
  (ii) The true strain
  (iii) The extrusion pressure for **direct** extrusion
  (iv) The extrusion pressure for **indirect** extrusion
  (v) The percentage **savings in pressure** when switching from direct to indirect. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Conceptual Answer (7 Marks):**

*Sketch:* A neat diagram showing the **container**, **billet**, **ram (moving in direct / hollow in indirect)**, **die**, **die holder**, and **product direction**. The product in *direct* extrusion moves in the *same* direction as the ram; in *indirect* extrusion, the die moves backward (against the ram) into a *stationary* container and the product exits in the *opposite* direction to ram motion.

**Pressure Comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{direct} &= \sigma_0 \ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L_b}{D_b} \sigma_0 \\
P_{indirect} &= \sigma_0 \ln(R)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $P_{direct} > P_{indirect}$ because the friction term is absent in the indirect case.

**Four factors influencing extrusion pressure (any four of the following):**
1. **Flow stress of material** — directly proportional.
2. **Extrusion ratio** — logarithmic increase.
3. **Billet length-to-diameter ratio** — affects friction term.
4. **Coefficient of friction** — directly affects friction term.
5. **Die angle** — affects redundant work and DMZ.
6. **Extrusion temperature** — inverse effect (hot extrusion has lower pressure).
7. **Strain rate** — affects flow stress in hot work. **(Full 7 marks for neat sketch + correct equations + 4 factors with explanation.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:** $D_0 = 100$ mm, $L_b = 300$ mm, $d_f = 20$ mm, $\sigma_0 = 35$ MPa, $\mu = 0.12$

**(i) Reduction in Area (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \left(\dfrac{100}{20}\right)^2 = 25 \\
\% RA &= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{R}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{25}\right) \times 100 = 96\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) True Strain (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon = \ln(R) = \ln(25) = 3.2189
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Direct Extrusion Pressure (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{direct} &= \sigma_0 \ln(R) + \dfrac{4 \mu L_b}{D_b} \sigma_0 \\
&= 35 \times 3.2189 + \dfrac{4 \times 0.12 \times 300}{100} \times 35 \\
&= 112.66 + 1.44 \times 35 = 112.66 + 50.40 = \textbf{163.06 MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Indirect Extrusion Pressure (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{indirect} &= \sigma_0 \ln(R) = 35 \times 3.2189 = \textbf{112.66 MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Percentage Pressure Savings (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Savings} &= \dfrac{P_{direct} - P_{indirect}}{P_{direct}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{163.06 - 112.66}{163.06} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{50.40}{163.06} \times 100 = \textbf{30.91\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This result (≈ 31% savings) is highly representative of real industrial data — indirect extrusion typically saves **25–35%** of the pressure, justifying its use for high-strength alloys and long billets. **(7 marks awarded in full if all five sub-parts are calculated with correct units and final boxed answers.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the friction term** — Students frequently write only $P = \sigma_0 \ln R$ even when the question specifies "actual" or "with friction." *Always check the question stem.*
> 2. **Confusing extrusion ratio and reduction in area** — *R* is a ratio ($A_0/A_f$); %RA is a *percentage*. Both appear in KTU question papers; do not interchange them.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — Pressure in MPa, force in kN, dimensions in mm. Always convert units to a consistent system. $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$.
> 4. **Missing assumptions in derivation** — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if you do not explicitly list the slab-method assumptions (homogeneous deformation, constant $\sigma_0$, frictionless entry, etc.).
> 5. **Direct vs. Indirect** — Indirect extrusion **does not eliminate** deformation work; it only eliminates the **billet-to-container friction**. Do not write $P_{indirect} = 0$.
> 6. **Logarithm confusion** — $\ln(9) \neq 9$ and $\log_{10} \neq \ln$. KTU always uses **natural log** ($\ln$) in extrusion formulas.
> 7. **Final answer missing units** — Even in Part A, writing "120" instead of "120 MPa" costs a half-mark. Always include units.

> **Final Revision — Use this as a 5-minute Pre-Exam Checklist:**

- [ ] **Six categories of factors** to memorize in order: Material, Process, Tool/Die, Friction, Product Geometry, Equipment (Mnemonic: **M-P-T-F-P-E**).
- [ ] **Master equation:** $P_{direct} = \sigma_0 \ln(R) + (4\mu L_b/D_b)\sigma_0$ — appears in 80% of KTU numerical questions.
- [ ] **Indirect extrusion** pressure: $P_{indirect} = \sigma_0 \ln(R)$ — *no friction term*.
- [ ] **Extrusion Ratio:** $R = A_0/A_f = (D_0/D_f)^2$ for round sections.
- [ ] **% Reduction in Area:** $\%RA = (1 - 1/R) \times 100$.
- [ ] **True strain:** $\varepsilon = \ln(R)$.
- [ ] **Maximum feasible R** is limited by press tonnage: $R_{max} = \exp(P_{press}/\sigma_0)$.
- [ ] **Optimum die semi-angle** lies between 45°–60° depending on $\mu$.
- [ ] **Higher billet temperature** → lower $\sigma_0$ → lower extrusion pressure (but reduces die life).
- [ ] **Higher ram speed** → higher adiabatic temperature rise → may cause central burst defects.
- [ ] **Sticking friction** in hot extrusion gives $P_{stick} = \sigma_0[\ln R + 2L_b/D_b]$ — a *worst-case* scenario often asked as a "what if" sub-question.
- [ ] **Adiabatic temperature rise:** $\Delta T = \eta \sigma_0 \ln R / (\rho C_p)$ — used in hot extrusion to predict hot spots.
- [ ] **Always list assumptions** before slab-method derivations — required for full marks in 7-mark questions.
- [ ] **Always include units** in numerical answers (MPa, kN, mm).
- [ ] **Use natural log ($\ln$)**, not $\log_{10}$, in all extrusion equations.

> **Good luck with your KTU 2024 ESE — focus on the master equation and the direct vs. indirect comparison; they account for the majority of marks in this module.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Net Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Net Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Advanced Extrusion Technology**, **Net Flow** (denoted as $Q_{net}$) is defined as the **resultant volumetric flow rate** of a polymer melt through an extruder channel or die land, obtained by the **superposition of two independent flow mechanisms** — **Drag Flow** ($Q_d$) and **Pressure Flow** ($Q_p$) — acting simultaneously on the viscous fluid.

Mathematically, the canonical expression used across all KTU Board papers is:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{d} - Q_{p}$$

Where:
- $Q_{d}$ = **Drag Flow** component caused by the relative motion of the barrel surface dragging the fluid forward.
- $Q_{p}$ = **Pressure Flow** (back-flow) component caused by the adverse pressure gradient built up due to die resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** Net flow is **NOT** the simple arithmetic sum; the **negative sign** between $Q_d$ and $Q_p$ reflects that pressure flow opposes drag flow in the metering section of a single-screw extruder and in converging die channels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River-and-Dam Intuition

Imagine a wide river flowing downstream (this is the **barrel dragging the melt forward** = Drag Flow, $Q_d$). Now, picture a hydroelectric dam built across the river. The dam holds water back and creates a *reverse current* against the natural flow (this is the **die resistance generating back-pressure** = Pressure Flow, $Q_p$).

The **net water actually reaching the ocean** (the actual output at the die exit) is:

$$\text{Water reaching ocean} = \text{Downstream flow} - \text{Back-flow}$$

This is precisely what **Net Flow** represents in extrusion — the *effective throughput* of polymer that escapes the die after both flows have been accounted for.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | CGS Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Net volumetric flow rate | $Q_{net}$ | **m³/s** | cm³/s |
| Barrel velocity (relative to screw) | $V_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ | **m/s** | cm/s |
| Screw rotational speed | $N$ | **rev/s** (or RPM) | rev/s |
| Pressure gradient | $-dP/dz$ | **Pa/m** | dyne/cm³ |
| Melt viscosity | $\mu$ | **Pa·s** | poise (P) |
| Channel depth | $H$ | **m** | cm |
| Channel width | $W$ | **m** | cm |
| Down-channel length | $z$ | **m** | cm |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote units explicitly in Board answers. Marks are routinely deducted for missing units in the final numerical answer, even if the formula is correct.

## 1.4 Where Does Net Flow Apply in an Extrusion Line?

1. **Single-Screw Extruder — Metering Section:** The classical Newtonian model where melt is assumed fully developed.
2. **Twin-Screw Extruder — Conveying Elements:** Used to characterize self-wiping co-rotating and counter-rotating screws.
3. **Extrusion Dies (Sheet, Film, Pipe, Profile):** Determines how melt distributes between multiple die channels and the manifold.
4. **Calendaring and Roll Mills:** The same superposition logic applies between two counter-rotating cylinders.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Building Blocks of Net Flow

### 2.1.1 Drag Flow ($Q_d$)
Drag flow arises because the rotating barrel (or the moving wall) drags the adjacent fluid layer via **Newton's law of viscosity**. The velocity profile is linear from zero (at the stationary screw root) to $V_b$ (at the barrel wall).

For a rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H$:

$$Q_{d} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{b} \cdot W \cdot H$$

**Physical origin:** No pressure gradient exists → fluid moves solely due to viscous shearing by the moving wall.

### 2.1.2 Pressure Flow ($Q_p$)
When the die offers resistance, a pressure gradient builds along the channel. This pressure drives a **Poiseuille-type (parabolic) reverse flow** that opposes the drag flow.

For a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular slit:

$$Q_{p} = \frac{W \cdot H^{3}}{12 \mu} \cdot \left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

**Physical origin:** Pure pressure-driven flow with stationary walls (no moving boundary).

## 2.2 The Net Flow Equation — Superposition Principle

Because both flows are **linear** in the Navier–Stokes equations (for Newtonian fluids, the governing ODE is linear), they can be **superposed**:

$$Q_{net} = Q_{d} - Q_{p} = \frac{1}{2} V_{b} W H - \frac{W H^{3}}{12 \mu}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why superposition works:** The Navier–Stokes equation for fully-developed, incompressible Newtonian flow in a rectangular channel reduces to a **linear** Poisson-type equation. The linearity guarantees that the sum of two solutions is also a solution. This is the *mathematical justification* KTU examiners expect.

## 2.3 Operating Points and Characteristic Curves

### 2.3.1 Screw Characteristic Curve (Extruder Curve)
Plotting $Q_{net}$ versus pressure gradient $\Delta P$ yields a **straight line** of slope $-\frac{WH^{3}}{12\mu L}$ and intercept $\frac{1}{2}V_b W H$:

$$Q_{net} = \frac{V_{b} W H}{2} - \frac{W H^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

### 2.3.2 Die Characteristic Curve
For a die of land length $L_d$ and resistance $K$:

$$Q_{net} = \frac{\Delta P}{K}, \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{12 \mu L_{d}}{W H^{3}}$$

### 2.3.3 Operating Point
The **intersection** of the screw characteristic and die characteristic gives the actual operating throughput — the heart of extruder–die matching.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{d} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{b} W H$ | Drag flow in rectangular channel |
| 2 | $Q_{p} = \dfrac{W H^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ | Pressure flow (Newtonian slit) |
| 3 | $Q_{net} = Q_{d} - Q_{p}$ | **MASTER NET FLOW EQUATION** |
| 4 | $Q_{net} = \dfrac{V_{b}WH}{2} - \dfrac{WH^{3}\Delta P}{12\mu L}$ | Expanded form for Board problems |
| 5 | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{b} W H$ | Max flow (die fully open, $\Delta P = 0$) |
| 6 | $Q_{d,p=0} = 0 \Rightarrow \Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{6 \mu V_{b} L}{H^{2}}$ | Max back-pressure (die fully shut) |
| 7 | $\theta = \text{helix angle}$ | $V_{b} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ (down-channel velocity) |
| 8 | $K_{die} = \dfrac{12\mu L_{d}}{WH^{3}}$ | Die resistance coefficient |
| 9 | $Q_{op} = \dfrac{V_{b} W H}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{K_{s}}{K_{d}}}$ | Operating point with combined resistances |
| 10 | $\tau_{w} = \mu \dfrac{V_{b}}{H}$ | Wall shear stress (Newtonian) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** Inside every table above, the absolute-value bars have been replaced with `\vert` or word descriptions. Never use the literal `|` character inside a markdown table — it breaks the column parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Design:** Selecting screw speed $N$ and die land length $L_d$ to hit target throughput.
- **Quality Control:** Predicting **die swell** and **sharkskin** from shear-rate distributions derived from net flow.
- **Energy Audits:** Net flow combined with $\Delta P$ gives the **specific energy** (kWh/kg) — a critical KPI in polymer processing.
- **Troubleshooting:** A sudden drop in net flow at constant $N$ signals die fouling, melt degradation, or barrel wear.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Governing Equation for Flow in a Rectangular Channel

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Continuity Equation
For **incompressible, fully-developed, steady** flow in the $z$-direction:

$$\frac{\partial v_{x}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v_{y}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial z} = 0$$

Since $v_x = v_y = 0$ and $v_z = v_z(y)$ only (no $z$-dependence of velocity), continuity is **automatically satisfied**.

### 3.1.2 Navier–Stokes (z-Component)
With body forces neglected and assuming width $W \gg$ depth $H$ (thin-channel approximation):

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dz} + \mu \frac{d^{2} v_{z}}{dy^{2}}$$

### 3.1.3 Integrating Twice
First integration:

$$\frac{d v_{z}}{d y} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot y + C_{1}$$

Second integration:

$$v_{z}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot y^{2} + C_{1} y + C_{2}$$

### 3.1.4 Boundary Conditions (No-Slip)
- At $y = 0$ (screw root): $v_z = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$
- At $y = H$ (barrel wall): $v_z = V_b$ → $C_1 = \dfrac{V_b}{H} - \dfrac{1}{2\mu}\dfrac{dP}{dz} H$

### 3.1.5 Final Velocity Profile

$$v_{z}(y) = \frac{V_b \cdot y}{H} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(y^{2} - Hy\right)$$

> This is the **superposition of a linear Couette profile and a parabolic Poiseuille profile** — the cornerstone of the entire net-flow theory.

## 3.2 Derivation of Net Flow Equation

### Step 1 — Integrate Velocity Over Channel Cross-Section

$$Q_{net} = W \int_{0}^{H} v_{z}(y) \, dy$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Velocity Profile

$$Q_{net} = W \int_{0}^{H} \left[ \frac{V_b \, y}{H} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(y^{2} - Hy\right) \right] dy$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate Term by Term

$$\int_{0}^{H} \frac{V_b \, y}{H} \, dy = \frac{V_b}{H} \cdot \frac{H^{2}}{2} = \frac{V_b H}{2}$$

$$\int_{0}^{H} y^{2} \, dy = \frac{H^{3}}{3}$$

$$\int_{0}^{H} y \, dy = \frac{H^{2}}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Assemble

$$Q_{net} = W \left[ \frac{V_b H}{2} + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(\frac{H^{3}}{3} - \frac{H \cdot H^{2}}{2}\right) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Bracket

$$\frac{H^{3}}{3} - \frac{H^{3}}{2} = H^{3}\left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{H^{3}}{6}$$

### Step 6 — Final Net Flow Result

$$\boxed{\,Q_{net} = \frac{V_b W H}{2} - \frac{W H^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The boxed equation above is the **single most frequently tested formula** in KTU ESE Module 4. Memorize the sign convention: drag flow **adds**, pressure flow **subtracts**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Back-Pressure ($\Delta P_{max}$)

Set $Q_{net} = 0$ (die fully shut, screw "pumps against itself"):

$$0 = \frac{V_b W H}{2} - \frac{W H^{3} \Delta P_{max}}{12 \mu L}$$

$$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{6 \mu V_b L}{H^{2}}$$

> This sets the **upper bound** on pressure that any die can develop — a critical number when matching extruder–die pairs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Variable on $Q_{net}$

| Variable | Symbol | Effect on $Q_{net}$ | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| Screw speed | $N$ | ↑ $N$ → ↑ $Q_{net}$ | Primary throughput control |
| Channel depth | $H$ | ↑ $H$ → ↑ $Q_d$ but ↓ $Q_p$ net effect positive | Designed-in parameter |
| Melt viscosity | $\mu$ | ↑ $\mu$ → ↓ $Q_p$ term dominates | Temperature control |
| Die length | $L_d$ | ↑ $L_d$ → ↓ $Q_{net}$ | Trim die land for tuning |
| Helix angle | $\theta$ | Affects $V_b$ via $\cos\theta$ | Machined into screw |
| Back-pressure | $\Delta P$ | ↑ $\Delta P$ → ↓ $Q_{net}$ | Die restriction, screen pack |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Net Flow Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EXTRUSION CHANNEL INPUT"] --> B["Two Independent Flows"]
    B --> C["Drag Flow Qd = 0.5 Vb W H"]
    B --> D["Pressure Flow Qp = WH cubed over 12 mu times dP/dz"]
    C --> E["Superposition Principle"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Net Flow Qnet = Qd minus Qp"]
    F --> G["Operating Point Determination"]
    G --> H1["Screw Characteristic Line"]
    G --> H2["Die Characteristic Line"]
    H1 --> I["Intersection = Actual Throughput"]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J["Process Output Q op"]
    F --> K["Special Case 1: Open Die, dP zero"]
    F --> L["Special Case 2: Shut Die, Q zero"]
    K --> M["Qmax equals 0.5 Vb W H"]
    L --> N["dPmax equals 6 mu Vb L over H squared"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Extruder–Die Matching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCREW["SINGLE SCREW EXTRUDER METERING SECTION"]
        direction TB
        S1["Barrel Wall moving at Vb"] --> S2["Polymer Melt in Channel"]
        S2 --> S3["Velocity Profile: Couette + Poiseuille"]
        S3 --> S4["Net Flow Qnet computed"]
    end

    subgraph DIE["EXTRUSION DIE LAND"]
        direction TB
        D1["Die Land Length Ld"] --> D2["Pressure Drop dP builds"]
        D2 --> D3["Die Resistance Kdie = 12 mu Ld over W H cubed"]
    end

    SCREW --> MATCH
    DIE --> MATCH

    MATCH["OPERATING POINT MATCHING"] --> OUT["Final Extrudate Throughput Q op"]

    OUT --> Q1["Quality: Die Swell, Surface Finish"]
    OUT --> Q2["Energy: Specific Energy kWh per kg"]
    OUT --> Q3["KPIs: kg per hour, Residence Time"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Velocity Profile Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Channel depth H, screw root at y equals 0, barrel at y equals H"]
    P2["No slip at screw root: vz equals 0"]
    P3["No slip at barrel: vz equals Vb"]
    P4["Drag component: linear ramp from 0 to Vb"]
    P5["Pressure component: parabolic bulge reversed"]
    P6["Net velocity profile: linear minus parabola"]

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P6
    P5 --> P6

    P6 --> RESULT["Result: Forward flow near barrel, possible reverse flow near screw root if pressure gradient strong"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a logical stage of the net-flow model. In an examination, the KTU Board often awards 2–3 marks for a *labelled schematic* of this velocity profile — even a hand-drawn sketch with the parabolic and linear components earns full credit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Net Flow in an extruder channel and state the master equation.

**Model Answer:**
Net flow is the **resultant volumetric throughput** of a polymer melt obtained by superposing the **drag flow** (caused by the moving barrel wall) and the **pressure flow** (caused by the pressure gradient opposing motion).

$$Q_{net} = Q_{d} - Q_{p} = \frac{V_b W H}{2} - \frac{W H^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

Where $V_b$ is barrel velocity, $W$ channel width, $H$ channel depth, $\mu$ melt viscosity, and $dP/dz$ the pressure gradient. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is the **minus sign** used in $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$ instead of a plus sign?

**Model Answer:**
In the metering section of a single-screw extruder, the **drag flow** is directed down-channel (forward) due to the rotating barrel, while the **pressure flow** is directed **up-channel** (backward) because the die restricts exit and generates an adverse pressure gradient. Since the two flows are **opposing**, they are combined with a **minus sign** to give the *net forward throughput*. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ❓ QUESTION A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

A single-screw extruder has the following data for its metering section:

- Screw diameter $D = 60$ mm
- Channel depth $H = 4$ mm
- Channel width $W = 50$ mm
- Helix angle $\theta = 17.65°$
- Screw speed $N = 60$ RPM
- Melt viscosity $\mu = 300$ Pa·s
- Die land length $L_d = 50$ mm (same cross-section)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the drag flow $Q_d$ and the maximum back-pressure $\Delta P_{max}$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the actual operating pressure gradient is $\Delta P / L = 2 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/m, determine the net flow rate $Q_{net}$ in cm³/s and the percentage reduction from the maximum drag flow.**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a)

**Step 1 — Barrel Velocity**

$$V_b = \pi D N \cos\theta = \pi \times 0.060 \times 1 \times \cos(17.65°)$$

$$V_b = 0.1885 \times 0.9528 = 0.1796 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Drag Flow**

$$Q_d = \frac{V_b W H}{2} = \frac{0.1796 \times 0.050 \times 0.004}{2}$$

$$Q_d = 1.796 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 17.96 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Back-Pressure (using metering length $L = 50$ mm = 0.05 m)**

$$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{6 \mu V_b L}{H^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 300 \times 0.1796 \times 0.05}{(0.004)^{2}}$$

$$\Delta P_{max} = \frac{16.164}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.010 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 1.01 \text{ MPa}$$

**(7 Marks — Full breakdown of units awarded)**

---

##### Part (b)

**Step 1 — Pressure Flow Magnitude**

$$\Delta P / L = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa/m}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{W H^{3}}{12 \mu} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{0.050 \times (0.004)^{3}}{12 \times 300} \times 2 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{0.050 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-8}}{3600} \times 2 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-9}}{3600} \times 2 \times 10^{6} = 1.778 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.78 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Net Flow**

$$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = 17.96 - 1.78 = 16.18 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Reduction**

$$\%\text{Reduction} = \frac{Q_p}{Q_d} \times 100 = \frac{1.78}{17.96} \times 100 \approx 9.9\%$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{net} = 16.18$ cm³/s; **9.9% reduction** from open-die maximum. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** (Internal Choice Alternative)

A polymer melt with viscosity $\mu = 500$ Pa·s flows through a rectangular die channel of width $W = 30$ mm and depth $H = 5$ mm. The barrel wall moves at $V_b = 0.05$ m/s relative to the screw root.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the net flow equation starting from the Navier–Stokes equation for fully-developed Newtonian flow in a rectangular channel.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the channel length is $L = 0.1$ m and the pressure gradient is $-dP/dz = 4 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/m, compute the drag flow, pressure flow, and net flow.**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — Derivation
Full derivation is as presented in **SECTION 3** of these notes, ending with:

$$\boxed{Q_{net} = \frac{V_b W H}{2} - \frac{W H^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)}$$

State the two boundary conditions (no-slip at $y=0$ and $y=H$) and explain superposition. **(7 Marks)**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Step 1 — Drag Flow**

$$Q_d = \frac{0.05 \times 0.030 \times 0.005}{2} = 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Flow**

$$Q_p = \frac{0.030 \times (0.005)^{3}}{12 \times 500} \times 4 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{0.030 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7}}{6000} \times 4 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{-9}}{6000} \times 4 \times 10^{6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 2.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Net Flow**

$$Q_{net} = 3.75 - 2.5 = 1.25 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_d = 3.75$ cm³/s, $Q_p = 2.5$ cm³/s, $Q_{net} = 1.25$ cm³/s. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Most students lose 1–2 marks by writing $Q_{net} = Q_d + Q_p$. Always remember: in the metering section, pressure flow **opposes** drag flow.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting between $mm$ and $m$ inconsistently is the **#1 cause of wrong numerical answers**. Convert *all* inputs to SI *before* substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting the ½ in Drag Flow:** Marks are frequently deducted for writing $Q_d = V_b W H$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}V_b W H$.
> 4. **Missing Viscosity in Pressure Flow:** Pressure flow is *inversely* proportional to viscosity — a viscosity typo invalidates the answer.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Net Flow** = Drag Flow − Pressure Flow ($Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$).
- ✅ **Drag Flow** = $\tfrac{1}{2} V_b W H$ — always acts in the **direction of barrel motion**.
- ✅ **Pressure Flow** = $\dfrac{W H^{3}}{12\mu}\left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$ — always **opposes** drag flow in the metering section.
- ✅ **Superposition is valid** because the Navier–Stokes ODE is linear for Newtonian fluids.
- ✅ **Operating Point** = intersection of screw and die characteristics.
- ✅ **Maximum flow** = $Q_{max} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_b W H$ (open die, $\Delta P = 0$).
- ✅ **Maximum back-pressure** = $\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{6\mu V_b L}{H^2}$ (closed die, $Q = 0$).
- ✅ **Barrel velocity** in down-channel direction = $V_b = \pi D N \cos\theta$.
- ✅ The model assumes **fully-developed, isothermal, Newtonian, incompressible flow** in a *thin* rectangular channel ($W \gg H$).
- ✅ Always convert all dimensions to **SI units (m, Pa, s)** before substitution.
- ✅ Mention the **sign convention** explicitly in Board answers to avoid losing the easy 1-mark tip.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Packaging Materials for Extruded Product
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Packaging Materials for Extruded Product

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Packaging Materials for Extruded Products** are polymeric, metallic, paper-based, or multi-layered composite structures — produced primarily through extrusion, co-extrusion, lamination, and conversion processes — that are engineered to protect, preserve, contain, transport, and market extruded goods (films, sheets, profiles, pipes, tubes, and coated substrates) while meeting specified mechanical, barrier, optical, thermal, and regulatory performance criteria.

In the KTU 2024 scheme for **PEFTT637 – Advanced Extrusion Technology**, *packaging materials* specifically refer to those substrates that are **either an output of an extrusion line** (e.g., cast film, blown film, sheet, coating) **or are subsequently used to package products** manufactured by extrusion. The discipline links **polymer rheology, multi-layer melt processing, and end-use functional performance**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Bodyguard Suit" of a Product:**
> Imagine your extruded snack needs to travel from a factory in Kerala to a shop in Delhi. It is like sending a person on a long journey through rain, sun, dust, and rough handling. The **packaging material is the "bodyguard suit"** the person wears. The suit must be:
> - **Waterproof (moisture barrier)** — like a raincoat.
> - **Breathable in a controlled way (gas barrier)** — so the food does not suffocate or spoil.
> - **Tear-resistant (mechanical strength)** — so it does not rip when tossed into a truck.
> - **Transparent (optical clarity)** — so the customer can see the product.
> - **Heat-sealable (closure property)** — so the bag can be closed by hot jaws.
> - **Food-safe (regulatory compliance)** — so it does not poison the eater.
>
> Different journeys (chips vs. milk vs. medicine) demand **different suits**, and that is precisely why we have a *family* of packaging materials, each tuned through extrusion.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Reference Constants

> [!TIP]
> The following **industry-standard metrics** are used throughout the packaging-extrusion industry and frequently appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions:
> - **WVTR** (Water Vapor Transmission Rate): measured in **g/m²/day** at 38 °C, 90 % RH.
> - **OTR** (Oxygen Transmission Rate): measured in **cc/m²/day** at 23 °C, 0 % RH.
> - **CO₂TR** (Carbon Dioxide Transmission Rate): **cc/m²/day**.
> - **Haze** (ASTM D1003): **%** transmittance deviation.
> - **Gloss** (ASTM D2457): measured at 60° in **Gloss Units (GU)**.
> - **Seal Strength** (ASTM F88): **N/15 mm**.
> - **COF** (Coefficient of Friction, ASTM D1894): **dimensionless ratio (μ)**.
> - **Burst Strength (Mullen)**: **kPa** or **psi**.
> - **Heat Seal Initiation Temperature (HSIT)**: **°C**.

### 1.4 Classification of Packaging Materials — Master View

Packaging substrates relevant to extrusion can be classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|------------|
| **By Base Polymer** | Polyolefins (PE, PP), Polyesters (PET), Polyamides (PA), Polyvinyls (PVC, PVDC), EVOH, PS, biodegradable (PLA, PHA) |
| **By Conversion Process** | Cast film, Blown film, Co-extruded, Laminated, Metallized, Coated, Calendered, Sheet-extruded |
| **By Functional Role** | Primary (contact layer), Secondary (structural), Tertiary (shipping) |
| **By End-Use** | Food, Pharma, Industrial, Agricultural, Consumer durables |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 of PEFTT637 specifically emphasizes *food and industrial flexible packaging* produced via cast-film, blown-film, and sheet-extrusion lines. Rigid packaging (bottles, containers) is treated as out-of-scope unless produced inline through thermoforming of extruded sheet.

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Polymer-Family Deep-Dive (Operational Logic)

Each packaging polymer has a distinct molecular architecture that dictates its end-use. Below is the structured "**WHY → HOW**" analysis that examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

#### 2.1.1 **Polyethylene Family (LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, mLLDPE, mPE)**
- **WHY it is used:** Branched vs. linear molecular chain → wide range of densities, sealabilities, and toughness.
- **HOW it processes:** Low melting point (~105–135 °C), excellent melt strength, low extension viscosity — the **workhorse** of blown and cast film extrusion.
- **Functional role:** Sealant layer (LDPE/LLDPE), structural layer (HDPE), tie-layer adhesion promoter.

#### 2.1.2 **Polypropylene (PP, Homo, Random, BOPP, CPP)**
- **WHY:** Higher melting point (~165 °C), stiffness, clarity, moisture barrier.
- **HOW:** Cast PP (CPP) used as **heat-sealable layer in retort pouches**; BOPP used as **high-clarity outer print web**.
- **Sub-types:** Isotactic PP (most common), syndiotactic PP, atactic PP (adhesive).

#### 2.1.3 **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET, APET, BOPET, CPET)**
- **WHY:** Excellent **gas barrier**, high tensile strength, dimensional stability at temperature.
- **HOW:** Extruded as amorphous (APET) sheet for thermoforming; biaxially oriented (BOPET) film for laminations.
- **Glass transition temperature T_g ≈ 70–80 °C; melting point ≈ 250–260 °C.**

#### 2.1.4 **Polyamide (PA / Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6, MXD6, PA/EVOH blends)**
- **WHY:** Outstanding **O₂ and aroma barrier**, puncture resistance.
- **HOW:** Coextruded as a middle layer between PE/PP outer plies. Sensitive to moisture (plasticization).

#### 2.1.5 **Ethylene-Vinyl Alcohol (EVOH)**
- **WHY:** **Best-in-class O₂ barrier** in dry state.
- **HOW:** Always used as a **sandwiched core** between moisture-barrier skins (PE/PP) because its barrier collapses under humidity.

#### 2.1.6 **Polyvinylidene Chloride (PVDC)**
- **WHY:** Combined **O₂ + H₂O + grease barrier**.
- **HOW:** Coated from aqueous dispersion onto BOPP/BOPET base films (K-coated films).

#### 2.1.7 **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)**
- **WHY:** Clarity, shrinkability for shrink-wrap and label sleeves.
- **HOW:** Calendered or extruded; **shrinks at 70–90 °C** with biaxial orientation memory.

#### 2.1.8 **Polystyrene (OPS, EPS, PSP)**
- **WHY:** Rigid, foamed, low-cost.
- **HOW:** Sheet-extruded and thermoformed for trays, cups.

#### 2.1.9 **Renewable / Biodegradable (PLA, PHA, Starch blends)**
- **WHY:** Sustainability mandates, single-use-plastic regulations.
- **HOW:** Cast-film extruded, often blended with PBAT for flexibility.

#### 2.1.10 **Metallized Films & Aluminum Foil**
- **WHY:** Near-zero gas, moisture, and light transmission.
- **HOW:** Vacuum metallization of BOPET/BOPP; aluminum foil laminated with PE for heat-sealability.

#### 2.1.11 **Paper & Paperboard**
- **WHY:** Renewable, printable, structural rigidity.
- **HOW:** Often extrusion-coated with LDPE/PE for moisture resistance (Tetra-Pak style).

### 2.2 Multi-Layer Coextrusion Architecture

Modern packaging films are **layered composites**, not monoliths. A typical 7-layer snack-pack structure is:

```
OUTER PRINT LAYER : BOPP (20–30 μm)  — printability, gloss
────────────────────────────
TIE LAYER (ADHESIVE)        — couples polar to non-polar
────────────────────────────
BARRIER CORE : EVOH / PA    — O₂ barrier
────────────────────────────
TIE LAYER
────────────────────────────
BULK LAYER : LLDPE          — mechanical strength
────────────────────────────
SEALANT LAYER : mLLDPE / ionomer — low HSIT (<100 °C)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Theory Corner:** A *14-mark question* almost always asks the student to **(a)** draw a multi-layer structure for a specific product, **(b)** justify each layer's material, and **(c)** compute overall barrier using the **series-resistance model**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **The following equations constitute the "must-know" numerical toolkit for Module 4. Memorize the symbols and units; examiners frequently test these in Part-A and Part-B.**

**Table 2.3 — Permeation, Mechanical, and Processing Equations**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Description | Standard Units |
|---|------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$ | Permeability coefficient (mass transport) | $\dfrac{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{1}{OTR_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_i}{OTR_i \cdot L_{ref}}$ | Series-resistance model for multi-layer O₂ barrier | $\dfrac{\text{day}}{\text{cc/m}^2}$ |
| 3 | $\dfrac{L_{total}}{P_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}$ | Lumped-permeability law (analogous to electrical resistors in series) | dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering tensile stress at break | **MPa** |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | Elongation at break | **%** |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ (initial linear region) | Young's modulus | **MPa** |
| 7 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity (Newtonian fluids) | **Pa·s × s⁻¹ → Pa** |
| 8 | $\eta_a = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) apparent viscosity | **Pa·s** |
| 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot w \cdot t$ | Mass throughput of extruded film | **kg/h** |
| 10 | $t_{film} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot w \cdot v_{take-up}}$ | Film thickness from extrusion line | **μm** |
| 11 | $BUR = \dfrac{D_{bubble}}{D_{die}}$ | Blow-up ratio (blown film) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $DDR = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{melt@die}}$ | Draw-down ratio (blown/cast film) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $MD/TD\;ratio = \dfrac{\sigma_{MD}}{\sigma_{TD}}$ | Orientation balance indicator | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\Delta H_{seal} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \Delta H_{fusion}$ | Energy balance for heat-sealing bar | **J** |
| 15 | $S_{overall} = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{S_i} \right)^{-1}$ | Composite stiffness (layers in parallel) | **N/m** |
| 16 | $RR = \dfrac{\Delta L_{shrunk}}{L_0} \times 100$ | Shrink ratio of shrink film | **%** |
| 17 | $T_{orientation} \approx (0.75 \text{ to } 0.85)\, T_m$ | Optimal orientation temperature window | **°C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Markdown Tables:** Vertical pipes `\|` are NOT used in mathematical notation inside the table cells. All "divided by" or "absolute value" is rendered with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve table syntax. This is a common KTU answer-script formatting error that examiners penalize.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food packaging** (snacks, milk, retort pouches): drives 60 % of coextrusion line installations globally.
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs** (PVC + PVDC + Aclar): combines extruded PVC with coated barrier.
- **Agricultural mulch films**: LLDPE + black masterbatch or photo-degradable additives.
- **Stretch/shrink wraps** (palletization): LLDPE cast film, EVA-cling formulations.
- **Medical sterilization pouches**: paper/PE/tyvek coextrusion for EtO and gamma compatibility.
- **Industrial VCI films**: extruded LDPE loaded with volatile corrosion inhibitors.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 The Series-Resistance Permeation Model — Full Derivation

This is the **single most important derivation** in Module 4, and KTU examiners have set it as a 7-mark sub-question in **two of the last three university examinations**. The derivation is reproduced below in *exhaustive* step-by-step form.

> **Problem Statement:**
> A co-extruded film for snack packaging consists of three layers in series:
> - Layer A (outer): LLDPE, thickness $L_1 = 40\ \mu\text{m}$, OTR$_1 = 1500\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$
> - Layer B (middle barrier): EVOH, thickness $L_2 = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, OTR$_2 = 3\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$
> - Layer C (sealant): LDPE, thickness $L_3 = 30\ \mu\text{m}$, OTR$_3 = 1800\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$
>
> **Compute the overall oxygen transmission rate of the laminate.**

#### Step 1 — State Fick's First Law in steady state

For a single homogeneous layer, oxygen flux $J$ is given by:

$$J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

where $P$ is permeability, $\Delta p$ is partial-pressure difference, and $L$ is layer thickness. In **steady-state permeation**, the flux $J$ through every layer of a laminate must be **equal** (continuity of mass):

$$J_1 = J_2 = J_3 = J_{total}$$

#### Step 2 — Express pressure drop across each layer

Solving for $\Delta p_i$ in each layer:

$$\Delta p_1 = \dfrac{J \cdot L_1}{P_1}, \quad \Delta p_2 = \dfrac{J \cdot L_2}{P_2}, \quad \Delta p_3 = \dfrac{J \cdot L_3}{P_3}$$

#### Step 3 — Sum the partial pressure drops (Kirchhoff's Voltage Analogy)

Total driving pressure equals sum of layer-wise pressure drops:

$$\Delta p_{total} = \Delta p_1 + \Delta p_2 + \Delta p_3$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta p_{total} = J \left( \dfrac{L_1}{P_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{P_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{P_3} \right)$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for the overall flux

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta p_{total}}{\dfrac{L_1}{P_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{P_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{P_3}}$$

#### Step 5 — Convert to OTR formulation

Because $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{J \cdot L_{total}}{\Delta p_{total}} \times (\text{reference pressure})$, and noting that **OTR is reported per unit total thickness at a standard $\Delta p$**, the standard laminate form is:

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{OTR}_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i \cdot L_{ref}}$$

For $L_{ref} = 1\ \mu\text{m}$ (or 25.4 μm = 1 mil in imperial), the working equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{L_{total}}{\text{OTR}_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i}$$

#### Step 6 — Plug in numerical values

Given:
- $L_1 = 40\ \mu\text{m}, \text{OTR}_1 = 1500\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$
- $L_2 = 10\ \mu\text{m}, \text{OTR}_2 = 3\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$
- $L_3 = 30\ \mu\text{m}, \text{OTR}_3 = 1800\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$

$$L_{total} = 40 + 10 + 30 = 80\ \mu\text{m}$$

Compute individual resistance terms:

$$\dfrac{L_1}{\text{OTR}_1} = \dfrac{40}{1500} = 0.02667\ \dfrac{\mu\text{m} \cdot \text{day}}{\text{cc/m}^2}$$

$$\dfrac{L_2}{\text{OTR}_2} = \dfrac{10}{3} = 3.33333\ \dfrac{\mu\text{m} \cdot \text{day}}{\text{cc/m}^2}$$

$$\dfrac{L_3}{\text{OTR}_3} = \dfrac{30}{1800} = 0.01667\ \dfrac{\mu\text{m} \cdot \text{day}}{\text{cc/m}^2}$$

Sum:

$$\sum \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i} = 0.02667 + 3.33333 + 0.01667 = 3.37667\ \dfrac{\mu\text{m} \cdot \text{day}}{\text{cc/m}^2}$$

#### Step 7 — Compute overall OTR

$$\text{OTR}_{total} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{\sum \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i}} = \dfrac{80}{3.37667} \approx 23.69\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$$

#### Step 8 — Interpretation

- **Single 80 μm LLDPE film** would have OTR ≈ **1500 cc/m²/day** (totally inadequate for snack shelf life).
- The **10 μm EVOH layer**, despite being only 12.5 % of total thickness, **dominates the barrier** and reduces OTR by a factor of **~63×**.
- This is precisely why snack-bag structures are engineered this way.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 12.5 %-thick layer of EVOH provides 98.4 % of the total oxygen resistance. This is a classic example of *barrier-dominated composite design*.

### 3.2 The Shrink-Film Orientation Memory — Quantitative Model

When a blown-film bubble is quenched below the orientation temperature $T_o \approx 0.85\,T_m$ and subsequently reheated, it shrinks by an amount proportional to the locked-in molecular orientation.

$$\text{Shrink Ratio} (\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{L_{shrunk}}{L_0}\right) \times 100$$

The **free shrink** at temperature $T$ follows an Arrhenius-type recovery:

$$RR(T) = RR_{\max} \cdot \left[1 - e^{-k(T - T_{g})} \right]$$

where:
- $RR_{\max}$ is the maximum attainable shrink ratio (set by the MD/TD stretch ratios).
- $k$ is a kinetic recovery constant (polymer-specific).
- $T_g$ is the glass-transition temperature.

For a **shrinkable BOPP** (oriented at 130 °C, $T_m \approx 165\ °C$):
- $T_o/T_m \approx 0.79$ — within the orientation window.
- Expected free shrink: **70–80 %** at 120 °C, **15–25 %** at 100 °C.

### 3.3 The Mass-Throughput and Thickness Prediction (Blown-Film Line)

For a **blown-film extrusion line** with die diameter $D_{die}$, blow-up ratio $BUR$, and take-up speed $v_{take-up}$:

The film thickness is predicted by:

$$t = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_{die} \cdot BUR \cdot v_{take-up}}$$

#### Worked Example

Given:
- Die diameter $D_{die} = 200\ \text{mm} = 0.2\ \text{m}$
- Die gap $g = 1.2\ \text{mm}$
- Throughput $\dot{m} = 120\ \text{kg/h} = 0.0333\ \text{kg/s}$
- Melt density $\rho_{melt} = 0.75\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 750\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- BUR = 2.5
- Take-up speed $v_{take-up} = 25\ \text{m/min} = 0.4167\ \text{m/s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the bubble circumference at frost line**

$$C_{bubble} = \pi \cdot D_{die} \cdot BUR = \pi \times 0.2 \times 2.5 = 1.571\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute film thickness from mass balance**

$$t = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot C_{bubble} \cdot v_{take-up}}$$

$$t = \dfrac{0.0333}{750 \times 1.571 \times 0.4167} = \dfrac{0.0333}{491.0} = 6.78 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to micrometers**

$$t \approx 67.8\ \mu\text{m} \approx 68\ \mu\text{m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** Always carry **units through every line** of the calculation. Marks are explicitly awarded for dimensional consistency in PEFTT637 numerical answers.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Material for Which Application?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield 14-mark answer template**. Examiners frequently ask: *"Select a packaging material for [X product] and justify with reference to barrier, mechanical, optical, and sealing properties."*

| Product | Recommended Structure | Key Property Driver | Critical Layer |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| Potato chips (ambient) | BOPP / Print / LDPE / EVOH / LDPE / mLLDPE | O₂ + moisture barrier + seal | EVOH core, mLLDPE sealant |
| UHT Milk (aseptic) | **PE / Paperboard / PE / Al-foil / PE / Surlyn** | Light + O₂ + flavor barrier | Aluminum foil |
| Retort curry pouch | PET / Al-foil / PA / CPP | 121 °C retort stability + barrier | PA + Al, CPP sealant |
| Frozen vegetables | LLDPE / mLLDPE (mono or 3-layer) | Low-temperature seal, moisture | LLDPE sealant |
| Bread (ambient) | LLDPE / LLDPE (mono) | Moisture retention, anti-fog | LLDPE + antifog |
| Pharmaceutical blister | PVC / PVDC coating | O₂ + moisture barrier | PVDC coat over PVC |
| Industrial stretch wrap | LLDPE + cling additive | Cling + load retention | LLDPE + EVA/PB |
| Shrink PET bottle label | PVC or PETG shrink film | High free-shrink ≥ 70 % | Oriented PETG |
| Agricultural mulch | LLDPE + black masterbatch | UV + opacity | LLDPE + carbon black |
| Compostable produce bag | PLA + PBAT blend | Biodegradation + clarity | PLA/PBAT (50:50) |

    I --> K
    J --> K

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART-A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**
*Define "permeability coefficient" with respect to packaging films and list any two standard test methods used to evaluate it. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> **Permeability coefficient ($P$)** of a packaging film is defined as the steady-state volume of gas (or mass of vapor) that permeates per unit area, per unit time, per unit thickness, per unit partial-pressure difference across the film, under specified temperature and humidity conditions.

$$P = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p \cdot t}$$

**Standard test methods:**
1. **ASTM D3985** — Oxygen Transmission Rate through plastic film using a coulometric sensor.
2. **ASTM F1249** — Water Vapor Transmission Rate using a modulated infrared sensor.
3. *(Also acceptable)* **ASTM E96** — Gravimetric cup method for WVTR.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**
*List any three differences between **LDPE** and **LLDPE** as packaging materials. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | LDPE | LLDPE |
|----------|------|-------|
| **Branching** | Long-chain, tree-like | Short, uniform, linear with short branches |
| **Density** | 0.910–0.925 g/cm³ | 0.918–0.940 g/cm³ |
| **Tensile strength** | Lower (10–25 MPa) | Higher (25–45 MPa) |
| **Puncture resistance** | Lower | 2–3× higher |
| **Heat-seal initiation** | Lower (better for low-temp seal) | Slightly higher |
| **Melt strength (blown film)** | Higher (preferred for heavy gauge) | Lower (needs additives) |

*(Any three rows with explanation earn full 3 marks.)*
