### 5.2 PART-B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]**

> **(a)** A flexible laminate used for packaging roasted coffee has the following structure:
>
> | Layer | Material | Thickness (μm) | OTR (cc/m²/day) |
> |-------|----------|----------------|------------------|
> | 1 | BOPET (outer) | 12 | 85 |
> | 2 | Aluminum foil | 9 | ≈ 0 |
> | 3 | LLDPE (sealant) | 60 | 1700 |
>
> Compute the **overall OTR** of the laminate and comment on whether it is suitable for a 12-month shelf life of roasted coffee (acceptance criterion: OTR ≤ 1 cc/m²/day). **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the role of **EVOH** as a barrier layer in multi-layer coextruded food packaging films. Why is it always placed as an **interior** layer, sandwiched between two polyolefin skins? Mention the effect of humidity on its barrier performance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

Apply the series-resistance model:

$$\dfrac{L_{total}}{\text{OTR}_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i}$$

$$L_{total} = 12 + 9 + 60 = 81\ \mu\text{m}$$

Compute each resistance:

$$\dfrac{L_1}{\text{OTR}_1} = \dfrac{12}{85} = 0.14118$$

$$\dfrac{L_2}{\text{OTR}_2} = \dfrac{9}{0} \to \infty \quad \text{(Al-foil is essentially a perfect barrier)}$$

$$\dfrac{L_3}{\text{OTR}_3} = \dfrac{60}{1700} = 0.03529$$

Since the Al-foil layer provides an **infinite resistance**, the series sum diverges to infinity:

$$\sum \dfrac{L_i}{\text{OTR}_i} \to \infty$$

Therefore:

$$\text{OTR}_{total} = \dfrac{81}{\infty} \to 0\ \text{cc/m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Conclusion:** The laminate has effectively **zero oxygen transmission rate**, far below the 1 cc/m²/day acceptance criterion. Hence, the structure is **fully suitable for 12-month coffee shelf life**, provided the Al-foil is pinhole-free and the seals are hermetic.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (a):** A common student error is to *ignore* the Al-foil layer and treat it like an EVOH barrier. Always state that the **vapor-deposited or laminated Al-foil is treated as a perfect barrier** ($P \approx 0$, $\text{OTR} \approx 0$), making the laminate's OTR effectively zero. Marks are deducted if this point is missing.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Role of EVOH:**
- Ethylene-Vinyl Alcohol copolymer is a **semicrystalline random copolymer** with the lowest oxygen permeability among commercially used packaging polymers.
- Its barrier arises from **strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding** between -OH groups, which reduces free volume and restricts O₂ molecule diffusion.
- Typical OTR of EVOH at 0 % RH is **1–5 cc·μm/m²·day**, ~10,000× better than LDPE.

**Why placed as interior core layer:**
1. EVOH is **moisture-sensitive** — water molecules plasticize the amorphous regions, breaking the hydrogen-bond network and dramatically increasing OTR (e.g., from 2 to 50 cc/m²/day as RH rises from 0 % to 100 %).
2. To protect EVOH from both **direct food moisture** and **ambient humidity**, it is sandwiched between two **polyolefin skins** (LDPE, LLDPE, PP) which act as moisture barriers.
3. **Tie layers** (e.g., maleic-anhydride grafted PE or EVA) are needed on each side of EVOH to bond it to the non-polar polyolefins.

**Humidity effect on barrier:**
- OTR of EVOH rises by a factor of **~25×** as RH increases from 0 % to 100 %.
- This is why EVOH is **never used as the outermost or innermost layer**; it must be **encapsulated**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (b):** Students often state "EVOH is moisture sensitive" but **fail to explain the molecular mechanism** (hydrogen-bond replacement by water clusters, plasticization). Examiners specifically test this conceptual depth. Award of 7 marks requires both the structural and mechanistic explanation.

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Expected Pattern]**

> **(a)** With the help of a labeled sketch, describe the **7-layer coextrusion feedblock** method for producing a multi-barrier packaging film. Identify the function of each layer for a typical snack-pack application. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **selection criteria for sealant-layer polymers** in flexible packaging. Compare **LDPE, LLDPE, mLLDPE, EVA, and ionomer (Surlyn)** as candidate sealant materials, citing at least two advantages and one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Feedblock coextrusion (block diagram description):**

A **feedblock** is a stainless-steel flow-distribution manifold that receives **molten polymer streams from 3–9 (or more) separate extruders** and arranges them into the desired layer sequence before the combined melt enters a flat or annular die.

For a 7-layer snack-pack film, the layer sequence (from outer to inner) is:

| Layer # | Material | Function |
|---------|----------|----------|
| 1 (outer) | **BOPP or LDPE** | Printability, gloss, abuse resistance |
| 2 | **Tie layer (MAH-grafted PE)** | Adhesion to barrier |
| 3 | **EVOH** | Oxygen barrier (dry-state) |
| 4 | **Tie layer** | Adhesion to polyolefin core |
| 5 | **PA (Nylon 6)** | Puncture + aroma barrier |
| 6 | **LLDPE** | Bulk mechanical layer |
| 7 (sealant) | **mLLDPE or ionomer** | Low-temperature heat seal, hermetic closure |

**Process sequence:**
1. Each polymer is **melted in its dedicated extruder**, filtered, and metered by a gear pump.
2. The streams enter the **feedblock** in the desired order.
3. The feedblock uses **flow-channel geometry and layer-multiplying elements** to build up the final 7-layer sandwich.
4. The combined melt stream passes through the **flat die** (or annular die for blown film) and is quenched on a chilled roll.
5. The resulting film is **wound, corona-treated**, and sent for printing and laminating if needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (a):** Students often draw a 3-layer or 5-layer structure and call it "7-layer." Examiners deduct marks if the **tie layers, EVOH, and PA are not all distinct** in the diagram and if the **function of each layer is not explicitly stated** in 1–2 lines.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Selection criteria for sealant-layer polymers:**

The sealant layer is the **innermost ply** of a flexible laminate and is responsible for:
1. **Hermetic closure** of the package via heat sealing.
2. **Low Heat-Seal Initiation Temperature (HSIT)** to protect outer barrier and printed layers.
3. **Hot-tack strength** — ability to hold the seal under load while still warm.
4. **Seal-through-contaminant** tolerance (e.g., grease, powder, fat).
5. **Food-contact compliance** (FDA 21 CFR / EU 10/2011).
6. **Good adhesion to adjacent tie layer or substrate.**

**Comparative Table — Sealant Polymer Candidates:**

| Polymer | HSIT (°C) | Hot-tack | Key Advantages | Key Limitation |
|---------|-----------|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **LDPE** | 105–115 | Moderate | Lowest cost, easy processing, good seal through contamination | Lower seal strength, narrower sealing window |
| **LLDPE** | 110–125 | High | High seal strength, good abuse resistance | Narrower sealing window vs LDPE |
| **mLLDPE (metallocene)** | 85–100 | High | Very low HSIT, excellent seal integrity, down-gauging | Higher cost, processing window sensitivity |
| **EVA (9–18 % VA)** | 80–95 | Moderate | Very low HSIT, broad sealing window, good seal through powder | Strong odor (acetic acid), limited barrier to grease |
| **Ionomer (Surlyn)** | 90–100 | Excellent | Outstanding hot-tack, oil/grease resistance, pinhole sealability | Highest cost, difficult to process, narrower operating window |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (b):** A common omission is the **failure to mention food-contact regulatory compliance** (FDA / EU) as a selection criterion. Examiners frequently allocate a 1-mark line item to "regulatory safety" — missing it costs a mark. Additionally, students often **omit hot-tack** as a separate property from cold-seal strength; this distinction is critical for high-speed form-fill-seal lines.

---

### 5.3 KTU Topic Recap & High-Yield Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this bulleted summary as a 5-minute pre-exam revision sweep.**

- [ ] **Packaging materials** are classified as polyolefins, barrier polymers, engineering polymers, and biodegradable.
- [ ] **LLDPE** dominates film extrusion due to high tensile and puncture strength at low density.
- [ ] **BOPP** is used for **printable outer webs** in laminates; **CPP** is the **sealant** in retort pouches.
- [ ] **EVOH** is the **best O₂ barrier** but **must be encapsulated** between polyolefin moisture-barrier skins.
- [ ] **Aluminum foil** acts as a **near-perfect barrier** ($P \approx 0$) to gases, moisture, and light.
- [ ] **PET (BOPET/APET)** offers high **dimensional stability** and is the workhorse for laminations and thermoforming.
- [ ] **PVC** is used for **shrink labels and clamshells**; clarity is excellent but environmental concerns are rising.
- [ ] **PA (Nylon)** provides **puncture + aroma barrier**; plasticizes with moisture.
- [ ] **PVDC coatings** (K-coat) enhance O₂ + H₂O barrier on BOPP/BOPET.
- [ ] **Multi-layer coextrusion** typically uses **3 to 9 extruders** feeding a **feedblock + die**.
- [ ] **Series-resistance model** is used to compute **laminate OTR** from individual layer OTRs.
- [ ] **Shrink films** use **oriented memory**, set at $T_o \approx 0.85\,T_m$; shrink ratio ≥ 70 % in BOPP.
- [ ] **Blow-up ratio (BUR)** and **draw-down ratio (DDR)** govern film orientation balance (MD vs TD).
- [ ] **Sealant layer** selection is based on **HSIT, hot-tack, seal-through-contaminant, and regulatory** compliance.
- [ ] **Sustainable alternatives** — PLA, PHA, PBAT blends — are emerging but have lower heat-resistance and barrier than fossil-based polymers.
- [ ] **Metallization** of BOPET/BOPP gives **near-zero OTR and WVTR** at low cost vs. aluminum foil lamination.
- [ ] **Retort pouches** must withstand **121 °C / 30 min sterilization** — only PA + CPP + Al combinations qualify.
- [ ] **Standard metrics to remember:** OTR, WVTR, COF, HSIT, hot-tack, haze, gloss, burst strength, seal strength.
- [ ] **Regulatory standards:** FDA 21 CFR, EU Regulation 10/2011, IS 14625 (India), FSSAI packaging rules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy Note:** In any 14-mark Module-4 question on packaging materials, structure your answer in the order **(i) → function/role, (ii) → material selection justification, (iii) → numerical calculation, (iv) → regulatory comment**. This four-part structure maps directly to the four mark clusters (3 + 4 + 4 + 3) that KTU examiners typically use, and it minimizes the risk of a "running out of marks" scenario.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recent Advances in Extrusion Technology: Carbon Dioxide or Nitrogen Assisted Extrusion Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT637 - ADVANCED EXTRUSION TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Recent Advances in Extrusion Technology: Carbon Dioxide or Nitrogen Assisted Extrusion Technology # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) or Nitrogen (N₂) Assisted Extrusion Technology** refers to a **Supercritical Fluid (SCF)-assisted polymer processing technique** in which a pressurized gas—either **supercritical carbon dioxide (scCO₂)** or **supercritical nitrogen (scN₂)**—is injected into the polymer melt inside an extruder barrel. The dissolved gas performs **three simultaneous engineering functions**: 1. **Plasticization** (reduction of melt viscosity and glass transition temperature, $T_g$). 2. **Swelling reduction** at the die exit (mitigating die-swell). 3. **Nucleation** of micro-cells when system pressure is rapidly released at the die, enabling **thermoplastic foaming**. The technique is classified under **physical foaming / processing-aid technologies** and is recognized as an environmentally benign alternative to conventional chemical blowing agents (CBAs) such as azodicarbonamide. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, students are expected to distinguish between CO₂- and N₂-assisted extrusion on the basis of critical constants, solubility parameters, plasticization efficiency, and resulting cell morphology. ## 1.2 Critical Properties of CO₂ and N₂ The selection between CO₂ and N₂ as the processing medium is dictated by their **supercritical constants** (temperature $T_c$ and pressure $P_c$): | Gas | $T_c$ (°C) | $P_c$ (MPa) | Solubility in Polymers | Plasticization Power | |-----|------------|-------------|------------------------|----------------------| | **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** | **31.1** | **7.38** | High (Lewis acid-base interaction) | Strong | | **Nitrogen (N₂)** | **−147.0** | **3.39** | Low (non-polar) | Weak | | Air | −140.7 | 3.77 | Low | Negligible | The mild $T_c$ of **CO₂ (31.1 °C)** is what makes it industrially preferred—it can reach the supercritical state at temperatures compatible with most thermoplastic processing windows. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soda Bottle Principle" Imagine a sealed plastic soda bottle (PET) containing carbonated water: - The **CO₂ molecules** are dissolved inside the liquid under high pressure (≈ 0.6 MPa at room temperature). - When you **slowly open the cap**, the pressure drops, but because it is slow, the CO₂ stays dissolved—no foam is formed. - When you **suddenly shake and pop the cap**, the sudden pressure drop triggers **violent nucleation**, producing a foamy mess. **CO₂-assisted extrusion works exactly in reverse:** - CO₂ is injected into the polymer melt (analogous to dissolving gas in the liquid). - It is **kept under high pressure** throughout the metering zone so it remains dissolved. - At the **die exit**, the pressure is intentionally released, triggering **controlled nucleation** of micro-cells. > [!NOTE] > The trick of the technology is **kinetic control**—keeping the gas dissolved inside the barrel, then releasing it abruptly at the die to produce a uniform, fine-celled foam or a viscosity-reduced extrudate. ## 1.4 Why Not Just Use Air? Air contains roughly **78 % N₂ and 21 % O₂**, but it is **not used** in commercial SCF extrusion because: - **O₂ causes oxidative degradation** of the polymer melt. - **N₂ alone requires extremely high pressures** (>30 MPa) to dissolve appreciably in most polymers because of its low solubility parameter. - CO₂ offers **Lewis acid–base interactions** with carbonyl groups (C=O) in polymers such as PET, PMMA, PLA, and PVC, dramatically improving solubility. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Six-Step Logic The SCF-assisted extrusion process is governed by **six sequential physical phenomena**: ### Step 1 — SCF Generation Liquid CO₂ (from a storage cylinder at ≈ 5–6 MPa) is pressurized beyond its **critical pressure (7.38 MPa)** and heated beyond its **critical temperature (31.1 °C)** to become supercritical. - Above the critical point, the fluid has **no meniscus**—gas-like diffusivity but liquid-like density. ### Step 2 — Metered Injection A precision **high-pressure syringe pump** or **piston pump** injects a **controlled mass fraction** ($w_{CO_2}$, typically 1–10 wt%) of scCO₂ into the extruder barrel, usually **downstream of the melting zone** (after the polymer is fully melted). ### Step 3 — Dissolution & Diffusion The injected SCF must **dissolve and diffuse** uniformly into the polymer melt. This is the **rate-limiting step** because polymer melts have very low diffusivity for gases. - A **static mixer (e.g., Saxton-type or Kenics)** is often inserted in the barrel to enhance mixing. ### Step 4 — Plasticization (Viscosity Reduction) Once dissolved, CO₂ molecules insert themselves between polymer chains, **increasing free volume** and **reducing chain entanglement density**. This causes: $$ T_g^{blend} = T_g^{polymer} \cdot m_2 $$ where $m_2$ is the mass fraction of polymer in the polymer–CO₂ system. A commonly used empirical correlation (Chow model) is: $$ \frac{T_g^{blend}}{T_g^{polymer}} \approx 1 - k \cdot w_{CO_2} $$ where $k \approx 0.10$–$0.30$ depending on polymer–gas pair. ### Step 5 — Pressure Drop at the Die The polymer–gas solution is forced through a die. The **die pressure** $P_d$ must be carefully controlled. If $P_d$ is too high, no foaming occurs; if too low, the gas escapes uncontrollably. ### Step 6 — Nucleation & Cell Growth Beyond the die, the rapid pressure drop causes **thermodynamic instability**. The dissolved gas **supersaturates**, and bubbles **nucleate homogeneously** within the matrix. Cell growth is then controlled by **die land length, temperature, and cooling rate**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas constitute the **high-yield set** for KTU 2024 scheme numerical and conceptual questions on this topic. | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Variables / Units | Validity / Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sanchez–Lacombe EoS (Pressure) | $P = -\frac{RT}{v}\left[\ln(1-\rho^*) + \left(1-\frac{1}{r}\right)\rho^*\right] - \rho^{*2}$ | $\rho^* = $ reduced density; $v = $ molar volume (m³/mol) | Predicts PVT behavior of polymer–CO₂ mixture | | 2 | Henry's Law (Solubility) | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | $k_H$ = Henry constant (mol/m³·Pa) | Valid at low pressures; $k_H^{CO_2} \gg k_H^{N_2}$ in most polymers | | 3 | Glass Transition Depression | $T_g^{blend} \approx T_g^{polymer}(1 - k \cdot w_g)$ | $w_g$ = gas mass fraction; $k \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ | Chow-type correlation | | 4 | Viscosity Reduction (Exponential) | $\eta_{mix} = \eta_0 \cdot \exp(-\alpha \cdot w_g)$ | $\alpha$ = plasticization coefficient | Empirical; $\alpha_{CO_2} \approx 4$–$8$ for PE/PS | | 5 | Cell Nucleation Rate (Classical) | $J = J_0 \exp\left(-\frac{16\pi \gamma^3}{3k_B T \Delta P^2}\right)$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension; $\Delta P$ = pressure drop | Higher $\Delta P$ $\Rightarrow$ exponentially higher $J$ | | 6 | Diffusion Coefficient (Free-Volume) | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{f_v}\right)$ | $f_v$ = free-volume fraction | $f_v$ increases with $w_{CO_2}$ | | 7 | Critical Pressure of Mixture | $P_{c,mix} = \sum x_i P_{c,i}$ | $x_i$ = mole fraction | Approximate; only qualitative | | 8 | Die Pressure Drop (Poiseuille) | $\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi R^4}$ | $L$ = land length; $Q$ = flow rate; $R$ = radius | For capillary/cylindrical die | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Packaging Industry**: Production of **PET foam trays** and **PP foam sheets** with densities as low as **30 kg/m³** for food packaging and automotive interiors. - **Wire & Cable Insulation**: CO₂-foamed LDPE insulation reduces material consumption by **20–35 %** while maintaining dielectric properties. - **Biodegradable Polymer Processing**: PLA and PHA are notoriously difficult to process—CO₂ plasticization **lowers melt viscosity by 50–80 %**, enabling extrusion at lower temperatures and reducing thermal degradation. - **Medical Devices**: scCO₂ is **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** by the FDA, making it suitable for medical-grade polymer processing. - **Environmental Benefit**: A single ton of foamed product can replace 2–3 tons of solid polymer, giving a **carbon footprint reduction of ≈ 40–60 %** per part. > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU valuation, students who mention the **environmental advantages** of CO₂ over **azodicarbonamide (chemical blowing agents)** consistently score higher in descriptive questions. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Drop Across the Die and Foam Cell Density A frequently asked numerical question in KTU examinations involves **relating the die pressure drop to the cell nucleation density** in the resulting foam. We derive the key relationship here, step by step. ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Classical Nucleation Theory (CNT) The homogeneous nucleation rate $J$ of cells in a polymer–gas solution is given by: $$ J = J_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{*}}{k_B T}\right) $$ where $\Delta G^{*}$ is the **Gibbs free-energy barrier** for forming a critical nucleus of radius $r^*$. ### 3.1.2 Expressing the Critical Free-Energy Barrier For a spherical gas bubble nucleating against surface tension $\gamma$ at pressure differential $\Delta P$: $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} $$ **Derivation logic (step-wise):** The work done in forming a spherical bubble of radius $r$ against a pressure difference $\Delta P$ and surface tension $\gamma$ is: $$ \Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta P + 4\pi r^{2} \gamma $$ To find the critical radius, set $\dfrac{d\Delta G}{dr} = 0$: $$ -4\pi r^{2} \Delta P + 8\pi r \gamma = 0 $$ Solving: $$ r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma}{\Delta P} $$ Substituting $r^{*}$ back into $\Delta G(r)$: $$ \Delta G^{*} = -\frac{4}{3}\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{\Delta P}\right)^{3}\Delta P + 4\pi \left(\frac{2\gamma}{\Delta P}\right)^{2}\gamma $$ $$ \Delta G^{*} = -\frac{32 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{3}} \Delta P + \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3}}{(\Delta P)^{2}} $$ $$ \Delta G^{*} = -\frac{32 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} + \frac{48 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} $$ $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} $$ ### 3.1.3 Substituting Back into the Nucleation Rate $$ J = J_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 k_B T (\Delta P)^{2}}\right) $$ ### 3.1.4 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Problem) > **Worked Example:** A polystyrene melt with dissolved CO₂ at **5 wt%** is extruded through a capillary die. The barrel pressure is **15 MPa** and the atmospheric pressure outside the die is **0.1 MPa**. The surface tension of the PS–CO₂ mixture is **0.020 N/m** and the melt temperature is **200 °C = 473 K**. Estimate the **relative nucleation rate** compared to a baseline $\Delta P$ of **10 MPa**. **Step 1 — Compute $\Delta P$:** $$ \Delta P = 15 - 0.1 = 14.9 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\Delta G^{*}$ (numerator):** $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi (0.020)^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} $$ At $\Delta P = 14.9$ MPa: $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times (14.9 \times 10^{6})^{2}} $$ $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{4.02 \times 10^{-4}}{6.66 \times 10^{14}} = 6.04 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$ At baseline $\Delta P = 10$ MPa: $$ \Delta G^{*}_{0} = \frac{16 \pi (0.020)^{3}}{3 (10 \times 10^{6})^{2}} = \frac{4.02 \times 10^{-4}}{3.0 \times 10^{14}} = 1.34 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} $$ **Step 3 — Ratio of nucleation rates:** $$ \frac{J}{J_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta G^{*} - \Delta G^{*}_{0}}{k_B T}\right) $$ Using $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K: $$ \Delta G^{*} - \Delta G^{*}_{0} = 6.04 \times 10^{-19} - 1.34 \times 10^{-18} = -7.36 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$ $$ k_B T = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 473 = 6.53 \times 10^{-21} \text{ J} $$ $$ \frac{J}{J_0} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{-7.36 \times 10^{-19}}{6.53 \times 10^{-21}}\right) = \exp(112.7) $$ $$ \frac{J}{J_0} \approx e^{112.7} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{48} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Insight:** A modest increase in $\Delta P$ from **10 MPa → 14.9 MPa** produces a **48-order-of-magnitude** increase in the nucleation rate. This is the *theoretical justification* for **rapid pressure drop** as the primary control knob for foam cell density in industrial SCF extrusion. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: CO₂ vs N₂ Assisted Extrusion > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important comparative table** for KTU 2024 examination questions on this topic. | Parameter | CO₂-Assisted Extrusion | N₂-Assisted Extrusion | |---|---|---| | Critical Temperature | **31.1 °C** (easy to achieve supercritical state) | **−147 °C** (requires cryogenic cooling) | | Critical Pressure | **7.38 MPa** | **3.39 MPa** | | Polymer Plasticization | **Strong** (Lewis acid–base interaction with C=O groups) | **Weak** (non-polar, minimal interaction) | | $T_g$ Depression | **Strong** (Δ$T_g$ up to 30–40 °C in PET) | **Negligible** | | Viscosity Reduction | **Significant** (η can drop by 50–80 %) | **Minor** (typically 10–25 %) | | Typical Loading (wt%) | 1–10 % | 1–5 % | | Cell Density in Foam | $10^{9}$–$10^{12}$ cells/cm³ | $10^{6}$–$10^{8}$ cells/cm³ | | Foam Morphology | **Fine, uniform, closed-cell** | **Coarser, often open-cell** | | Cooling Efficiency | Limited (latent heat effects) | Better (acts as heat sink) | | Cost of Gas | Low (recyclable, by-product of fermentation/ammonia) | Moderate (requires cryogenic supply) | | Environmental Impact | **Excellent** (no VOC, no residue) | Good | | Typical Applications | PET/PVC foam, PLA processing, viscosity reduction | Polyolefin foam, large structural foams, wire insulation | | Process Complexity | **High** (sealing, recycling loop needed) | **Very High** (cryogenic + high pressure) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of a CO₂-Assisted Extrusion Line Below is a **block-level functional architecture** of a typical CO₂-assisted extrusion line. The flow proceeds from gas storage through the extruder and out to the foamed product. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CO2 Cylinder
5-6 MPa Liquid] --> B[CO2 Cooler
Below 25 C] B --> C[High Pressure Pump
Syringe / Piston Type] C --> D[Heater Band
Heats to Supercritical State] D --> E[Static Mixer
Inject at 10-25 MPa] E --> F[Extruder Barrel
Metering Zone] F --> G[Static Mixer In Barrel
Dissolution Zone] G --> H[Die Assembly
Capillary or Slit] H --> I[Pressure Drop Zone
Foam Nucleation] I --> J[Cooling / Calibration Unit] J --> K[Haul Off and Cutting] K --> L[Finished Foamed Profile] P[Hopper and Feeder
Polymer Pellets] --> F Q[Gas Recycle Loop
Pressure Regulator] --> A ``` ## 4.2 Pressure Profile Along the Extruder Axis A typical pressure profile along the length of the extruder in SCF-assisted extrusion: ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Feed Zone
0.1 MPa Atmospheric] --> P2[Melting Zone
5-8 MPa] P2 --> P3[CO2 Injection Point
8-10 MPa] P3 --> P4[Mixing Dissolution Zone
10-15 MPa] P4 --> P5[Metering Zone
15-20 MPa] P5 --> P6[Die Inlet
12-15 MPa] P6 --> P7[Die Exit
Sudden Drop to 0.1 MPa] ``` ## 4.3 Process Control Architecture (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SCFSystem["SCF Supply System"] SCF1[Cylinder] SCF2[Pump] SCF3[Pre-Heater] SCF1 --> SCF2 --> SCF3 end subgraph ExtruderSystem["Extruder System"] EX1[Hopper] EX2[Plasticating Barrel] EX3[Mixing Section] EX4[Metering and Die] EX1 --> EX2 --> EX3 --> EX4 end SCF3 --> EX3 subgraph ControlSystem["Process Control"] CTRL1[PLC Controller] CTRL2[Pressure Transducers] CTRL3[Temperature Sensors] CTRL4[Gas Mass Flow Meter] end EX4 --> CTRL1 SCF2 --> CTRL4 CTRL2 --> CTRL1 CTRL3 --> CTRL1 CTRL4 --> CTRL1 ``` > [!NOTE] > In the schematic, the **CO₂ injection point is located at the downstream end of the melting zone** but **upstream of the metering zone**. This is the standard industrial arrangement because the polymer must be in a homogeneous melt state before SCF introduction. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] **Define the term "supercritical fluid" with respect to CO₂. Why is supercritical CO₂ preferred over gaseous CO₂ in extrusion processing?** **Model Answer (Expected Length: 6–8 lines):** A supercritical fluid (SCF) is a substance existing **above both its critical temperature ($T_c$) and critical pressure ($P_c$)**, possessing properties intermediate between a gas (low viscosity, high diffusivity) and a liquid (high density, good solvating power). For CO₂, the critical point is at **$T_c = 31.1 °C$** and **$P_c = 7.38$ MPa**. Supercritical CO₂ is preferred over gaseous CO₂ because: 1. Its **density is liquid-like (≈ 700–900 kg/m³)**, providing superior solvation and dissolution into the polymer melt. 2. Its **diffusivity is gas-like (≈ $10^{-3}$ cm²/s)**, ensuring rapid mass transfer into the polymer matrix. 3. By tuning **pressure and temperature slightly above the critical point**, the solvent power and density can be **continuously adjusted**—a property unavailable with subcritical gases. 4. Above the critical point there is **no gas–liquid meniscus**, eliminating two-phase flow instabilities in the barrel. *(Self-Valuation: 3 marks = 1 mark for correct critical constants + 2 marks for justification of advantages)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper] **List three differences between CO₂- and N₂-assisted extrusion.** **Model Answer:** | S. No. | CO₂-Assisted | N₂-Assisted | |--------|--------------|-------------| | 1 | $T_c = 31.1 °C$, easily supercritical | $T_c = -147 °C$, requires cryogenic cooling | | 2 | Strong plasticization; $\Delta T_g$ up to 30 °C | Weak plasticization; $\Delta T_g$ ≈ 2–5 °C | | 3 | Produces fine closed-cell foams (10⁹–10¹² cells/cm³) | Produces coarse, often open-cell foams | *(Self-Valuation: 3 marks = 1 mark per difference, 3 × 1 = 3)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **Note:** Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation, the student must answer **either Question A OR Question B** in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of plasticization and viscosity reduction** of a polymer melt by supercritical CO₂. Use appropriate equations and describe how the plasticization effect depends on the **operating pressure and temperature**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe a **complete industrial setup for CO₂-assisted extrusion**, including the SCF generation unit, injection system, extruder modifications, and downstream handling. Draw a labeled schematic and discuss **two safety considerations** specific to handling high-pressure CO₂. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Mechanism of Plasticization:** When supercritical CO₂ dissolves into a polymer melt, three sequential effects occur: **1. Free-Volume Increase:** CO₂ molecules occupy interstitial positions between polymer chains. The free-volume fraction $f_v$ increases, allowing greater segmental mobility. $$ f_v^{blend} \approx f_v^{polymer} + \beta \cdot w_{CO_2} $$ **2. Glass Transition Depression:** Increased chain mobility manifests as a reduction in $T_g$: $$ T_g^{blend} = T_g^{polymer} \left(1 - k \cdot w_{CO_2}\right) $$ with $k \approx 0.10$–$0.30$ depending on the polymer–CO₂ pair. **3. Viscosity Reduction (Shear-Thinning Compounding):** The dissolved gas acts as a molecular lubricant, reducing entanglement friction: $$ \eta_{mix}(T, P, \dot{\gamma}) = \eta_{polymer}(T, P, \dot{\gamma}) \cdot \exp(-\alpha \cdot w_{CO_2}) $$ where $\alpha$ is the plasticization coefficient, empirically in the range **4–8** for PE and PS at typical extrusion shear rates. **Dependence on Pressure and Temperature:** - **Higher pressure** (10–25 MPa) increases gas solubility, hence enhances plasticization. Solubility follows Henry's Law at moderate pressures: $C = k_H \cdot P$. - **Higher temperature** *decreases* solubility (exothermic dissolution) but *increases* polymer free-volume. The **net effect** on viscosity is non-monotonic—there is an optimum temperature window (typically 10–40 °C *above* the polymer $T_m$ or $T_g$). **Numerical Example for KTU:** At **8 MPa** and **180 °C**, **5 wt% CO₂ in PS** reduces viscosity by approximately **70 %** compared to pure PS at the same temperature and shear rate. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Industrial Setup Description:** The complete line consists of the following subsystems: 1. **CO₂ Storage & Conditioning:** - Liquid CO₂ cylinders at 5–6 MPa. - Pre-cooler to maintain liquid state below 25 °C. - Sub-micron particulate filter to protect the pump. 2. **High-Pressure Pump (SCF Pump):** - Typically a **syringe pump or piston pump** capable of 30–50 MPa discharge. - Flow rate controlled to within ± 1 % accuracy. 3. **SCF Heater & Static Mixer:** - Heats CO₂ to **above 31.1 °C** to ensure supercritical state. - Inline static mixer ensures homogeneous gas injection. 4. **Modified Extruder Barrel:** - Injection port located **2/3 down the barrel** (after melting, before metering). - High-pressure-rated **static mixing elements** (Kenics, Saxton) installed downstream of injection port. - Pressure transducers at three locations: injection point, mid-barrel, die. 5. **Die Assembly:** - Capillary or slit die with **thermostatic control**. - Pressure drop rate is the critical control variable. 6. **Downstream Cooling & Calibration:** - Air-cooling or water-spray calibration units. - Foam expansion ratio of **5–40×** achievable. **Two Safety Considerations:** (i) **Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs):** All high-pressure lines (>10 MPa) must have dual PRVs set at 110 % of working pressure to prevent over-pressurization. (ii) **CO₂ Asphyxiation Hazard:** In confined spaces, leaked CO₂ can displace oxygen. Continuous **O₂ sensors** must be installed near the pump and extruder head. (iii) **Thermal Runaway during Depressurization:** Rapid release of dissolved CO₂ can cause frost formation (Joule–Thomson cooling). The expansion valve must be **temperature-controlled**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper] **(a)** Using classical nucleation theory, derive the expression for the **critical radius of a gas nucleus** in a polymer–CO₂ solution. Hence, calculate the **critical cell radius** for a system where surface tension is **0.025 N/m** and the pressure drop across the die is **12 MPa**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of N₂-assisted extrusion compared to CO₂-assisted extrusion. Mention **three specific industrial applications** of N₂-assisted foaming. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Derivation of Critical Radius:** Consider a spherical gas bubble of radius $r$ forming in a polymer–gas solution. The total Gibbs free-energy change is: $$ \Delta G(r) = -\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \Delta P + 4 \pi r^{2} \gamma $$ The first term is the **bulk free-energy release** (favorable, proportional to volume); the second term is the **surface free-energy penalty** (unfavorable, proportional to area). **Critical Condition** ($d\Delta G/dr = 0$): $$ \frac{d\Delta G}{dr} = -4 \pi r^{2} \Delta P + 8 \pi r \gamma = 0 $$ Solving: $$ r^{*} = \frac{2 \gamma}{\Delta P} $$ **Numerical Calculation:** Given $\gamma = 0.025$ N/m and $\Delta P = 12 \times 10^{6}$ Pa: $$ r^{*} = \frac{2 \times 0.025}{12 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{0.050}{12 \times 10^{6}} $$ $$ r^{*} = 4.167 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 4.17 \text{ nm} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The critical radius is only **≈ 4 nm**—smaller than the radius of a polymer chain. This is why CO₂ nucleates **micro-cellular** foams with cell densities of $10^{9}$–$10^{12}$ cells/cm³. **Substituting $r^*$ back into $\Delta G$** yields the activation barrier: $$ \Delta G^{*} = \frac{16 \pi \gamma^{3}}{3 (\Delta P)^{2}} = 3.64 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$ --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Advantages of N₂ over CO₂:** 1. **Chemically inert** — no risk of hydrolytic degradation in moisture-sensitive polymers (e.g., PC, PA). 2. **Lower gas cost per unit mass**, though throughput is lower. 3. **No acidic interaction** with polymers—safer for engineering plastics that hydrolyze. 4. **Good for open-cell foam structures** where high open-cell content is desired. **Limitations:** 1. Requires **cryogenic cooling** to $-147 °C$ to reach supercritical state — high energy cost. 2. **Poor plasticization** — viscosity reduction limited to ≈ 10–25 %, often insufficient for high-viscosity polymers like PEEK. 3. **High operating pressures** (>30 MPa) needed for sufficient solubility. 4. **Coarser cell morphology** — average cell diameters of 50–500 µm versus 1–50 µm for CO₂. **Three Industrial Applications of N₂-Assisted Foaming:** 1. **Cross-linked polyethylene (XLPE) foam sheets** for automotive NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) insulation. 2. **High-density polyethylene (HDPE) foam boards** for marine dock floats and subsea buoyancy modules. 3. **Polypropylene (PP) foam trays** for automotive interior door panels and bumper cores. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the units** of critical pressure (MPa, not bar) — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 mark for missing units in numerical answers. > 2. **Writing "supercritical gas" instead of "supercritical fluid"** — examiners *do* deduct marks because "gas" implies below-critical state. > 3. **Confusing N₂ and CO₂ critical temperatures** — many students write $T_c$ of N₂ as 31.1 °C. Verify before writing! > 4. **Skipping the Henry's Law** when explaining solubility — examiners expect a *quantitative* anchor, not just qualitative "CO₂ dissolves better." > 5. **Not drawing a labeled schematic** in Part B — even a simple block diagram with **at least 5 labeled components** earns full diagram marks (typically 2 of 7 marks). > 6. **Omitting safety considerations** in CO₂ setup questions — this is a frequently tested CO (Course Outcome) and students lose 1–2 marks for missing it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:** - ✅ **CO₂ critical point: 31.1 °C, 7.38 MPa.** N₂ critical point: −147 °C, 3.39 MPa. - ✅ **Supercritical CO₂ combines gas-like diffusivity with liquid-like density** — that is the *root reason* it plasticizes polymers. - ✅ The **plasticization effect lowers $T_g$** and reduces **melt viscosity exponentially** with gas mass fraction. - ✅ **Henry's Law** governs solubility at moderate pressures: $C = k_H P$. - ✅ **Classical Nucleation Theory** gives critical radius $r^* = 2\gamma / \Delta P$. - ✅ **Higher $\Delta P$ at the die** $\Rightarrow$ exponentially higher cell density (≈ 48 orders of magnitude for ~50 % $\Delta P$ increase). - ✅ **CO₂-assisted extrusion** is preferred for **fine-cell, closed-cell foams** and **viscosity reduction** of difficult polymers (PLA, PET, PVC). - ✅ **N₂-assisted extrusion** is preferred for **chemically sensitive polymers** and **coarse, open-cell foams**. - ✅ **Industrial setup components** to remember: Cylinder → Cooler → Pump → Heater → Injector → Static Mixer → Die → Foam. - ✅ **Safety**: Pressure relief valves, O₂ sensors, Joule–Thomson cooling handling. - ✅ **Environmental advantage** of CO₂: Replaces chemical blowing agents like azodicarbonamide; **carbon footprint reduction ≈ 40–60 %**. - ✅ **Free-volume theory** explains *why* viscosity drops: CO₂ chains up free volume, increasing polymer chain mobility. ---
